Professor View 01 / 108
Business English -- 50 Aulas

Eduarda Gabriel

De "eu travo quando a conversa fica longa" a falar com confiança em calls de reestruturação e Chapter 11

B1+ (Intermediário) São Paulo, SP Investment Banking Associate / Houlihan Lokey 60 min / Online / 1x semana
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Eduarda Gabriel
27 anos
Investment Banking Associate / Houlihan Lokey
São Paulo, SP (pais em Jundiaí)
B1+ (Intermediário — habilidades passivas fortes, fluência oral fraca)
Business English — Reestruturação, Chapter 11, M&A
50 aulas de 60 min
Online (Zoom) — 1x por semana

Jornada de Transformação

De: Associada que lê e escreve bem, entende tudo, mas trava quando precisa falar

Para: Profissional que participa ativamente de reuniões internacionais, defende posições em inglês com precisão jurídico-financeira em 50 aulas

Promessa do Programa

"De 'eu travo quando a conversa fica longa' a falar com confiança em calls de reestruturação e Chapter 11"

Forças

  • Leitura avançada — lê documentos complexos em inglês com facilidade
  • Listening corporativo — compreende calls e apresentações em inglês
  • Autoconsciência dos gaps — sabe exatamente onde precisa melhorar
  • Vocabulário passivo rico — conhece muitas palavras, mas não usa ativamente
  • Alta motivação — carreira exige inglês fluente diariamente
  • Escrita funcional — produz e-mails e memos com apoio de IA

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Fluência em tempo real — trava em conversas longas ou inesperadas
  • Conjugação verbal — confunde tempos verbais ao falar
  • Dependência de IA para escrita — não produz textos sozinha com confiança
  • Pronúncia em velocidade — fala lentamente e com sotaque forte
  • Nominalização e colocações — dificuldade com chunks naturais
  • Gestão de turnos — não sabe quando/como entrar na conversa

Currículo -- 50 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Diagnostic Session — Who Is Eduarda GabrielPresent simple + Present continuousConference role-playRecord 90-sec intro
2Holding the Floor — Joining the ConversationTurn-taking phrases + present simple questionsMeeting simulationRecord 2-min response to prompt
3Numbers That Tell Stories — Financial Data in EnglishLarge numbers, percentages, comparisonsData presentation drillWrite 3 sentences with financial data

5Review & Simulation — First Client CallAll structures from 1-4Full call simulation (20 min)Reflection audio
6Ice-Breaking in International SettingsSocial English + question formsNetworking role-playRecord ice-breaking practice

8Defending a Position — Arguing with DataFirst conditional + opinion framesDebate simulationRecord opinion on case study
9Summarizing & Clarifying in Real TimeReporting verbs + paraphrasingMeeting summary taskWrite meeting summary email
10Review & Simulation — Strategy MeetingAll structures from 6-9Full meeting simulationReflection audio

12Creditor Negotiations — Language of CompromiseModal verbs (could/would/might)Negotiation role-playRecord negotiation practice
13DIP Financing — Explaining Complex StructuresCause and effect connectorsExplanation drillWrite process explanation
14Cross-Border M&A — Multi-party DiscussionsThird conditional + complex sentencesMulti-party simulationCase study analysis
15Review & Simulation — Restructuring CallAll structures from 11-14Full restructuring call simReflection audio

23Persuasion Techniques in Business EnglishPersuasive phrases + rhetorical questionsPitch simulationRecord persuasive argument

26Valuation Language — Multiples, DCF, ComparablesTechnical finance vocabularyValuation explanation drillWrite valuation summary

28Term Sheets & LOIs — Reading and DiscussingLegal document vocabularyDocument review discussionAnnotate sample term sheet
29Reporting to Senior PartnersFormal reporting structuresPartner briefing simulationRecord executive summary

31Conference Introduction & Elevator PitchPitch structure + impact phrasesElevator pitch practiceRecord 60-sec pitch
32Panel Discussions — Contributing ActivelyAgreement/disagreement + supportingPanel simulationWatch panel + analyze
33Networking Follow-ups — Building RelationshipsFollow-up email + social languageNetworking chain practiceWrite follow-up emails
34Industry Events — Technical PresentationsPresentation language + Q&A handlingFull presentation deliveryRecord 5-min presentation
35Review & Simulation — Industry ConferenceAll conference structuresFull conference simulationReflection audio
36Leading a Meeting — Setting Agenda & Next StepsMeeting management languageMeeting leadership practiceWrite meeting agenda
37Giving Constructive FeedbackFeedback frameworks + diplomatic languageFeedback role-playRecord feedback delivery
38Managing Conflict in EnglishDe-escalation phrases + empathy languageConflict resolution simWrite conflict response
39Mentoring Junior Team MembersCoaching language + encouragementMentoring role-playRecord mentoring session

41IELTS/TOEIC Overview — Structure & StrategyTest-specific strategiesPractice test sectionsComplete practice section

Welcome, Eduarda!

Onboarding

Bem-vinda ao programa! Antes de cada aula, complete as atividades de Pre-class. Aqui estão 5 frases de emergência que você pode usar a qualquer momento:

1 Could you repeat that, please? Poderia repetir, por favor?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain? Não tenho certeza se entendi. Poderia explicar?
3 Let me think about that for a moment. Deixe-me pensar um momento.
4 In my experience, I would say... Na minha experiência, eu diria...
5 Could I add something to that? Posso acrescentar algo a isso?
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Eduarda Gabriel?

Diagnostic + Self-Introduction: Present simple & Present continuous
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Restructuring -- the process of reorganizing a company's financial structure
"Houlihan Lokey is one of the leading firms in restructuring."
Deal -- a business agreement or transaction between parties
"Eduarda is working on a new deal in São Paulo."
Counterpart -- a person in a similar position at another company
"My counterpart in New York coordinates the creditor meetings."
Advise -- to give professional guidance or recommendations
"We advise clients on Chapter 11 restructuring."
Currently -- at the present time; right now
"I am currently focused on a major restructuring case."
Contribute -- to actively participate or add value to a discussion
"I want to contribute more actively in English calls."
Stakeholder -- a person or group with an interest in a company's decisions
"The stakeholders approved the restructuring plan last week."
Walk Through -- to explain something step by step
"Let me walk you through the deal structure."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Restructuring
Deal
Counterpart
Advise
Currently
Contribute
Stakeholder
Walk Through

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda works at Houlihan Lokey as an investment banking associate. She advises clients on restructuring and Chapter 11 processes. Every week, she coordinates with her counterpart in New York. She is currently working on a major deal involving Brazilian creditors. The stakeholders are reviewing the new proposal today. Eduarda wants to contribute more actively in English calls and she is preparing for an important presentation. Her manager often asks her to walk the team through the financial analysis.

1. What does Eduarda do every week?
A She is preparing a presentation.
B She coordinates with her counterpart in New York.
C She is reviewing the new proposal.
2. Which sentence describes something happening right now (present continuous)?
A She advises clients on restructuring.
B The stakeholders are reviewing the new proposal today.
C She works at Houlihan Lokey.
3. Why does Eduarda want to improve her English?
A To contribute more actively in English calls.
B To coordinate with creditors in Brazil.
C To work at the New York office.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = facts, habits, routines (fatos, hábitos, rotinas — coisas permanentes ou regulares)
Present Continuous = actions happening now or around now (ações acontecendo agora ou no período atual)

TenseStructureExample
Present SimpleSubject + verb (base/s)"She works at Houlihan Lokey."
Present ContinuousSubject + am/is/are + verb-ing"She is working on a deal."

Common Mistake:

"She is working at Houlihan Lokey." (para fato permanente)

"She works at Houlihan Lokey." (present simple para fatos permanentes)

Use Present Simple para fatos permanentes e Present Continuous para ações temporárias ou em progresso.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or verb form.

"Eduarda at Houlihan Lokey as an associate."
"She is working on a restructuring deal."
"Her coordinates the creditor meetings every week."
"The team clients on complex financial transactions."
"She wants to to the discussion in English."
"The are reviewing the new proposal today."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I am currently working on restructuring deals and Chapter 11 processes."
? "Good morning. I am Eduarda Gabriel from the São Paulo team."
? "I look forward to contributing to this call."
? "My role involves advising clients and coordinating with counterparts."
? "I am an investment banking associate at Houlihan Lokey."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

I am an investment banking associate at Houlihan Lokey in São Paulo.
I am currently working on restructuring deals and Chapter 11 processes.
My role involves advising clients and coordinating with counterparts.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are joining a restructuring call for the first time. The lead asks: "Who just joined?" What is the best response?
A "I am Eduarda. I do deals."
B "Hi, I am Eduarda Gabriel, an associate on the restructuring team in São Paulo."
C "Hello, I work in Brazil."
2. A colleague asks: "What are you working on right now?" What is the correct answer?
A "I work on a restructuring deal since January."
B "I am currently working on a major restructuring case."
C "I am work on a deal now."
3. You want to participate in the discussion. What do you say?
A "I want say something."
B "Can I talk now?"
C "I would like to contribute to this discussion."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are joining a new restructuring call at Houlihan Lokey. Introduce yourself to the team. Include: your name, your role, what your team does, and one sentence about the type of deals you work on.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1 I am Eduarda Gabriel. I work at Houlihan Lokey. Eu sou Eduarda Gabriel. Trabalho na Houlihan Lokey.
2 I am currently working on a restructuring deal. Estou atualmente trabalhando em um deal de reestruturação.
3 Could you walk me through the deal structure? Poderia me explicar a estrutura do deal?
4 I would like to contribute to this discussion. Gostaria de contribuir para esta discussão.
5 The main stakeholders have approved the plan. Os principais stakeholders aprovaram o plano.
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

Holding the Floor

Joining the Conversation: Turn-taking phrases & Present simple questions
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

INTERRUPT -- to politely break into a conversation to add your point
"Sorry to interrupt, but I have an update on the deal."
CLARIFY -- to make something clearer or easier to understand
"Could you clarify what you mean by the recovery timeline?"
ELABORATE -- to explain something in more detail
"Could you elaborate on the restructuring proposal?"
AGENDA -- a list of topics to be discussed in a meeting
"The first item on the agenda is the creditor proposal."
FOLLOW UP -- to continue or check on something discussed earlier
"I will follow up with the legal team on Friday."
PERSPECTIVE -- a particular way of thinking about something
"From my perspective, the deal needs more time."
PROPOSAL -- a formal suggestion or plan presented for consideration
"The creditors submitted a new proposal yesterday."
CONSENSUS -- general agreement among a group of people
"Do we have a consensus on the next steps?"

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Interrupt
Clarify
Elaborate
Agenda
Follow Up
Perspective
Proposal
Consensus

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda joins a weekly restructuring call with the New York team. The team lead goes through the agenda and asks if anyone has updates. Eduarda wants to contribute, so she interrupts politely: "If I may, I would like to share a quick update." She clarifies the timeline and elaborates on the creditor proposal. James asks: "Do you have consensus on the Brazilian side?" Eduarda responds: "Not yet, but I will follow up with the legal team." She shares her perspective and the team agrees on next steps.

1. What does Eduarda do when she wants to contribute?
A She waits until the meeting ends.
B She interrupts politely and shares an update.
C She sends an email after the call.
2. What question does James ask using present simple?
A "Are you having consensus on the Brazilian side?"
B "Do you have consensus on the Brazilian side?"
C "Did you have consensus on the Brazilian side?"
3. How does Eduarda respond when there is no consensus yet?
A She ignores the question and changes topics.
B She says they already agreed on everything.
C She says she will follow up with the legal team.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple Questions = perguntas com do/does para fatos, opiniões e rotinas
Turn-taking Phrases = frases educadas para entrar na conversa ou pedir a palavra

StructureFormulaExample
Present Simple Question (do)Do + subject + base verb?"Do you agree with the proposal?"
Present Simple Question (does)Does + subject + base verb?"Does the team have a consensus?"

Turn-taking Phrases:

PhraseUse
"Could I add something?"Pedir a palavra educadamente
"If I may..."Introduzir sua opinião com cortesia
"Sorry to interrupt, but..."Interromper educadamente
"Going back to what you said..."Retomar um ponto anterior
"To build on that point..."Adicionar algo ao que foi dito

Common Mistake:

"Do she agree with the proposal?"

"Does she agree with the proposal?" (use does para he/she/it)

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

" you agree with the restructuring proposal?"
" the team have a consensus on the timeline?"
"Could I add something to the ?"
"If I may, I would like to on that."
"I will with the legal team before the next call."
"From my , the proposal needs more time."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "From my perspective, we need to clarify two key points."
? "If I may, I would like to share an update."
? "I will follow up with the legal team and share the details by Friday."
? "Sorry to interrupt, but I have new information on the proposal."
? "Could you elaborate on the recovery timeline before I continue?"

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

If I may, I would like to share my perspective on the deal.
Sorry to interrupt, but I think we need to clarify the timeline.
Do we have a consensus on the proposal, or should I follow up?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are in a meeting and someone is presenting. You have important information to add. What do you say?
A "Stop talking. I have something to say."
B "Sorry to interrupt, but I have an update on this topic."
C "I talk now, okay?"
2. The meeting is ending and you want to suggest a follow-up. What do you say?
A "We need to meet again. Schedule it."
B "Okay, bye everyone."
C "Before we end, could I suggest we follow up on the timeline next week?"
3. A colleague said something you did not fully understand. What do you say?
A "Could you clarify what you mean by that?"
B "I do not understand nothing."
C "What? Say again."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are in a weekly restructuring call. Your team lead asks for updates. Practice sharing your update, then asking the team a question.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1 Could I add something to this point? Posso acrescentar algo a esse ponto?
2 If I may, I would like to share my perspective. Se me permite, gostaria de compartilhar minha perspectiva.
3 Sorry to interrupt, but I think we need to clarify this. Desculpe interromper, mas acho que precisamos esclarecer isso.
4 Going back to what you said about the deal... Voltando ao que você disse sobre o deal...
5 Do we have a consensus on this proposal? Temos um consenso sobre esta proposta?
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

Numbers That Tell Stories

Financial Data in English: Large numbers, percentages & comparisons
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

REVENUE -- the total income generated by a business
"Houlihan Lokey's restructuring revenue grew by twelve percent this year."
DECLINE -- to decrease in value or amount
"The company's revenue declined by eight percent in Q2."
ESTIMATE -- an approximate calculation or judgment
"We estimate the deal value at approximately two hundred million dollars."
FORECAST -- a prediction of future trends based on data
"The forecast shows a recovery in the second half of the year."
LEVERAGE -- the use of borrowed money to increase potential returns
"The company's leverage ratio is higher than the industry benchmark."
MARGIN -- the difference between costs and selling price; profit margin
"The operating margin improved from fifteen to twenty percent."
QUARTER -- a three-month period in a financial year (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4)
"Revenue in the third quarter exceeded our forecast."
BENCHMARK -- a standard or reference point for comparison
"We use industry benchmarks to evaluate the deal."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Revenue
Decline
Estimate
Forecast
Leverage
Margin
Quarter
Benchmark

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda presents the quarterly numbers to the restructuring team. Last quarter, revenue declined by eight percent. The team estimates the deal value at approximately two hundred million dollars. The forecast shows improvement in Q4. Compared to the industry benchmark, the company's leverage ratio is higher. However, the operating margin improved from fifteen to twenty percent. Eduarda explains: "While revenue declined in Q2, the margin is now higher than our initial estimate."

1. What happened to revenue last quarter?
A Revenue increased by eight percent.
B Revenue declined by eight percent.
C Revenue stayed the same.
2. How does the company's leverage compare to the industry?
A It is lower than the benchmark.
B It is higher than the benchmark.
C It is equal to the benchmark.
3. What improved from fifteen to twenty percent?
A The revenue.
B The leverage ratio.
C The operating margin.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Large Numbers / Números Grandes
1,000one thousand
1,000,000one million
1,000,000,000one billion
1,000,000,000,000one trillion
$2.5Btwo point five billion dollars
Percentages / Porcentagens

Use BY to describe changes:
"Revenue increased by fifteen percent." / "A receita aumentou em quinze por cento."
"Costs decreased by two percent." / "Os custos diminuíram em dois por cento."
"Profits grew by three percent year-over-year." / "Os lucros cresceram três por cento ano a ano."

Comparisons / Comparações

"higher than" = mais alto que
"compared to" = comparado a
"as opposed to" = em oposição a
"while X increased, Y declined" = enquanto X aumentou, Y diminuiu

Common Mistake / Erro Comum

"The revenue declined in fifteen percent."
"The revenue declined by fifteen percent."
Em inglês, mudanças percentuais usam "by", não "in".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"___ declined by eight percent in the third quarter."
"We ___ the deal value at approximately two hundred million dollars."
"The ___ shows a recovery in Q4."
"The operating ___ improved from fifteen to twenty percent."
"Compared to the industry ___, our leverage is higher."
"Revenue in the third ___ exceeded our forecast."

Stage 2: Ordering

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "Compared to Q2, revenue declined by eight percent."
? "Good morning. Let me walk you through the Q3 numbers."
? "The forecast shows revenue growing by approximately five percent in Q4."
? "Revenue in the third quarter was approximately one hundred and fifty million."
? "However, the operating margin improved from fifteen to twenty percent."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Revenue declined by eight percent in the third quarter.
We estimate the deal value at approximately two hundred million dollars.
Compared to the industry benchmark, our margin improved significantly.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A colleague asks you to present Q3 results. What is the best way to start?
A "The numbers are bad this quarter."
B "Revenue in the third quarter was approximately one hundred and fifty million."
C "I think the revenue is around 150."
2. How do you describe a decrease from $200M to $184M?
A "Revenue declined in eight percent."
B "Revenue declined by eight percent."
C "Revenue declined for eight percent."
3. How do you compare your company's margin to the industry?
A "Our margin is higher than the industry benchmark."
B "Our margin is more higher than the industry."
C "Our margin is high than the benchmark."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Present the financial results of an imaginary company. Include: revenue, margin, and a comparison to last quarter. Try to use: "declined by", "higher than", "approximately", and "forecast".

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1 Revenue increased by fifteen percent in Q3. A receita aumentou quinze por cento no terceiro trimestre.
2 Compared to last quarter, the margin is higher. Comparado ao último trimestre, a margem é maior.
3 We estimate the deal value at approximately two hundred million. Estimamos o valor do deal em aproximadamente duzentos milhões.
4 The forecast shows a decline in the second half. A projeção mostra uma queda no segundo semestre.
5 Let me walk you through the numbers. Deixe-me apresentar os números para vocês.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

The Deal Room

Restructuring Vocabulary Mastery: Collocations & Present Perfect
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key expressions for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

CLOSE A DEAL -- to finalize a business agreement
"We closed the deal with the creditors last Friday."
FILE FOR BANKRUPTCY -- to formally request legal protection from creditors
"The company has filed for bankruptcy under Chapter 11."
REACH AN AGREEMENT -- to come to a mutual decision after negotiation
"The parties have reached an agreement on the payment terms."
CONDUCT DUE DILIGENCE -- to investigate a company before a transaction
"We have conducted due diligence on the target company."
SUBMIT A PROPOSAL -- to formally present a plan for consideration
"The advisory team has submitted a proposal to the board."
NEGOTIATE TERMS -- to discuss conditions until both sides agree
"Eduarda has negotiated the terms with the US counterparts."
RAISE CAPITAL -- to obtain funding from investors or lenders
"The company needs to raise capital to fund the restructuring."
ISSUE A REPORT -- to publish or release a formal document
"We have issued the preliminary report to all stakeholders."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Close a Deal
File for Bankruptcy
Reach an Agreement
Conduct Due Diligence
Submit a Proposal
Negotiate Terms
Raise Capital
Issue a Report

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda has been working on a major restructuring case at Houlihan Lokey. The company has filed for bankruptcy under Chapter 11. Eduarda's team has conducted due diligence and has submitted a preliminary proposal to the creditors. They have negotiated the terms for three weeks, but they have not reached an agreement yet. The board wants to raise capital, and the advisory team has already issued a report with three scenarios. Eduarda tells James: "We have closed similar deals before -- this time will not be different."

1. What has the company filed for?
A The company has filed a report.
B The company has filed for bankruptcy under Chapter 11.
C The company filed for a new proposal.
2. Have they reached an agreement yet?
A Yes, they reached an agreement last week.
B No, they have not reached an agreement yet.
C They do not want to reach an agreement.
3. Which verb form appears most in this text?
A Past simple (filed, closed, negotiated).
B Present perfect (has filed, has conducted, have negotiated).
C Present continuous (is filing, is conducting).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Present Perfect / Presente Perfeito

Structure / Estrutura: Subject + have/has + past participle (3rd column)

Affirmative / Afirmativa"We have closed the deal."
Negative / Negativa"We have not closed the deal."
Question / Interrogativa"Have we closed the deal?"
Time Markers / Marcadores Temporais

already = já (frase afirmativa) | yet = já/ainda (frase negativa ou pergunta)
just = acabou de | recently = recentemente
since = desde (ponto no tempo) | for = por/há (duração)
ever = alguma vez | never = nunca

Common Mistake / Erro Comum

"We closed the deal since January."
"We have been working on the deal since January."
Com "since" e "for", use present perfect, nunca past simple.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We ___ an agreement with the creditors."
"The company ___ for bankruptcy last month."
"Eduarda ___ the terms for three weeks."
"Have you ___ due diligence yet?"
"The team ___ the proposal."
"They ___ enough capital yet."

Stage 2: Ordering

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "We have submitted a proposal to the creditors."
? "The company has filed for bankruptcy under Chapter 11."
? "We have closed the deal with the creditors."
? "We have conducted due diligence on the target company."
? "We have negotiated the terms and reached an agreement."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

We have conducted due diligence on the target company.
The parties have reached an agreement on the restructuring terms.
Have you submitted the proposal to the board yet?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A colleague asks if the team has completed the investigation. What collocation do you use?
A "We have closed a deal on the investigation."
B "We have conducted due diligence on the target company."
C "We have issued an investigation to the team."
2. Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect?
A "We have closed the deal last Friday."
B "We have already closed the deal."
C "We have close the deal."
3. How do you say the team formally sent a plan to the board?
A "The team has submitted a proposal to the board."
B "The team has raised a proposal to the board."
C "The team has issued a proposal to the board."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Describe a deal or project you have worked on recently. What steps have you completed? What have you not finished yet? Try to use: "have conducted", "have submitted", "have not reached", "have already".

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1 We have closed the deal with the creditors. Fechamos o deal com os credores.
2 The company has filed for bankruptcy under Chapter 11. A empresa entrou com pedido de recuperação judicial sob o Chapter 11.
3 Have you conducted due diligence yet? Você já conduziu a due diligence?
4 We have not reached an agreement yet. Ainda não chegamos a um acordo.
5 The team has already submitted the proposal. A equipe já submeteu a proposta.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

First Client Call

Review & Simulation: All structures from Aulas 1-4 integrated
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Review Cards

Vocabulary

Review the grammar structures and examples.

RESTRUCTURING -- reorganizing a company's structure or finances
"The restructuring process has been complex."
COUNTERPART -- a person in a similar position at another organization
"My counterpart in New York has already reviewed the proposal."
CONTRIBUTE -- to give input or add value to a discussion
"If I may, I would like to contribute to this discussion."
CONSENSUS -- general agreement among a group
"Have we reached a consensus on the timeline?"
REVENUE -- income generated from business activities
"Revenue declined by eight percent compared to last quarter."
FORECAST -- a prediction about future financial performance
"The forecast shows improvement, but we have not closed the deal yet."
CONDUCT DUE DILIGENCE -- to investigate a company before a transaction
"We have conducted due diligence on the target."
NEGOTIATE TERMS -- to discuss conditions until both sides agree
"Eduarda is currently negotiating the terms with creditors."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Restructuring
Counterpart
Consensus
Revenue
Forecast
Conduct Due Diligence
Negotiate Terms
Contribute

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda works at Houlihan Lokey and is currently working on a major restructuring case. She advises clients on Chapter 11 and coordinates with her counterparts in New York. Last week, her team submitted a proposal. They have conducted due diligence and have negotiated the main terms, but they have not reached a final agreement yet. In the last call, Eduarda interrupted politely to clarify the timeline: "If I may, I would like to elaborate on the forecast. Revenue declined by eight percent in Q2, but the margin is now higher than the industry benchmark. We estimate a recovery in Q4." Do the stakeholders have a consensus on the next steps? James asked Eduarda to follow up and issue a report by Friday.

1. Which grammar structure does "is currently working" represent?
A Present Simple
B Present Continuous
C Present Perfect
2. "They have conducted due diligence" is an example of:
A Past Simple -- a finished action with a specific time
B Present Perfect -- a completed action relevant to now
C Present Continuous -- something happening right now
3. Why does Eduarda say "If I may" before sharing information?
A Because she is unsure about the data.
B Because she wants to end the meeting.
C Because it is a polite turn-taking phrase to join the conversation.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comprehensive Review

Grammar

Review of all grammar structures from the program.

Structure When to Use Example
Present Simple Facts, routines, general truths "She works at Houlihan Lokey."
Present Continuous Current projects, temporary actions "She is currently negotiating."
Present Simple Questions Asking for information "Do you have a consensus?"
Turn-taking Joining conversations politely "If I may, I would like to add..."
Numbers/Comparisons Presenting data and comparing "Revenue declined by 8%."
Present Perfect Completed actions relevant to now "We have conducted due diligence."

Common Mistake Compilation

✗ "She work at Houlihan Lokey." ✓ "She works at Houlihan Lokey." (Present Simple: add -s for he/she/it)
✗ "She is work on a deal." ✓ "She is working on a deal." (Present Continuous: be + verb-ing)
✗ "We have conduct due diligence." ✓ "We have conducted due diligence." (Present Perfect: have + past participle)
✗ "Revenue declined in eight percent." ✓ "Revenue declined by eight percent." (Use "by" with percentages)
✗ "I want say something." ✓ "If I may, I would like to..." (Polite turn-taking)

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form.

"She at Houlihan Lokey."
"The team currently the terms."
" you due diligence yet?"
"Revenue eight percent in Q2."
"If I , I would like to elaborate."
"We have the proposal to the board."

Stage 2: Client Call Steps

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? Present the latest financial data and comparisons.
? Introduce yourself and state your role.
? Confirm consensus and next steps.
? Describe what has been done (due diligence, proposals).
? Describe your current project and what you are working on.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Practice these sentences that combine multiple grammar structures.

We have conducted due diligence, and revenue declined by eight percent compared to last quarter.
If I may, I would like to walk you through the forecast — we estimate a recovery in Q4.
Have you reached a consensus on the proposal, or should we follow up with the stakeholders?

Stage 4: Integrated Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to tell a client what your team has done so far. Which sentence is correct?
A "We conduct due diligence and submit the proposal."
B "We have conducted due diligence and submitted the proposal."
C "We are conducting due diligence and submitting the proposal."
2. You are presenting Q3 revenue data. How do you describe an 8% drop?
A "Revenue declined in eight percent."
B "Revenue has declined for eight percent."
C "Revenue declined by eight percent compared to Q2."
3. You want to politely add information during a meeting. What do you say?
A "I want to say something now."
B "If I may, I would like to elaborate on the forecast."
C "I am elaborating on the forecast."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are about to join your first client call in English at Houlihan Lokey. The client is a US company that has filed for Chapter 11. Prepare a 2-minute briefing: introduce yourself, present the latest numbers, describe what has been done, and ask the client a question.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5 (Master Card)

1 I am Eduarda Gabriel, an associate at Houlihan Lokey. Sou Eduarda Gabriel, associada na Houlihan Lokey.
2 Could you walk me through the deal structure? Poderia me apresentar a estrutura do deal?
3 If I may, I would like to share my perspective. Se me permite, gostaria de compartilhar minha perspectiva.
4 Revenue declined by eight percent compared to Q2. A receita caiu oito por cento em relação ao segundo trimestre.
5 We have conducted due diligence and submitted the proposal. Conduzimos a due diligence e submetemos a proposta.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

Ice-Breaking in International Settings

Social English & question forms: start conversations and find common ground
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key social expressions for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Break the Ice -- to start a conversation in a relaxed, friendly way
"I always break the ice with a simple question."
Small Talk -- light, informal conversation about general topics
"We made small talk about the weather before the meeting."
Mingle -- to move around and talk to many different people at an event
"She likes to mingle at networking receptions."
Common Ground -- a shared interest, opinion, or experience
"We found common ground in our love of finance."
Acquaintance -- someone you know a little, but not well
"He is a business acquaintance from a conference."
Strike Up -- to start a conversation with someone
"I struck up a conversation with the person next to me."
Get Along -- to have a friendly, easy relationship with someone
"I get along well with my international colleagues."
Outgoing -- friendly and confident in social situations
"You need to be outgoing at networking events."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each expression with its definition.

Break the Ice
Small Talk
Mingle
Common Ground
Acquaintance
Strike Up
Get Along
Outgoing

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

It is the first evening of the Restructuring Summit, and Eduarda is at the networking reception. She is a little nervous, but she knows how to break the ice. She walks up to an acquaintance and asks, "Do you come here every year?" Soon they are making small talk about the city. Eduarda likes to mingle, so she strikes up a conversation with a delegate from London. "What brings you to São Paulo?" she asks. "Have you been here before?" They quickly find common ground: they both work on cross-border deals. Eduarda is outgoing tonight, and she gets along well with everyone she meets.

1. What does Eduarda do to start conversations?
A She waits for people to talk to her.
B She breaks the ice and strikes up conversations.
C She talks only about deals.
2. Which question uses the present perfect (Have + been)?
A "Do you come here every year?"
B "Have you been here before?"
C "What brings you to São Paulo?"
3. What do Eduarda and the London delegate have in common?
A They both work on cross-border deals.
B They both live in London.
C They are old friends.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Question forms = in English, the auxiliary (do/does, am/is/are, have/has) comes before the subject (em inglês, o auxiliar vem ANTES do sujeito na pergunta)

TypeStructureExample
Yes/No (be)Am/Is/Are + subject + ...?"Are you enjoying the event?"
Yes/No (do)Do/Does + subject + verb?"Do you work in finance?"
Wh- questionWh- + auxiliary + subject + verb?"What do you do?"
Present perfectHave/Has + subject + past participle?"Have you been here before?"

Common Mistake:

"What you do for work?" / "You are from where?"

"What do you do for work?" / "Where are you from?"

Nunca esqueça o auxiliar (do/are/have): sem ele, a pergunta soa como tradução direta do português.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each question with the correct auxiliary or question word.

" brings you to São Paulo?"
" you know the host of the event?"
" you been to this conference before?"
"Where you from?"
"What you think of the keynote?"
"How you know Sofia?"

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the ice-breaking conversation in the correct order. Listen first.

? "Oh, restructuring is my field too. Have you been to São Paulo before?"
? "Hi, I do not think we have met. I am Eduarda."
? "It was great talking to you. Let's stay in touch."
? "We have a lot in common. Here is my card."
? "What brings you to the conference?"

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each question, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Is this your first time at the Restructuring Summit?
What brings you to São Paulo this week?
Have you worked on any cross-border deals?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to start a conversation with someone standing alone at the reception. What is the best opener?
A "Who are you?"
B "Hi, I do not think we have met. Is this your first time at the summit?"
C "You are from where?"
2. You want to ask what someone does for work. Which question is correct?
A "What you do for work?"
B "What work you do?"
C "What do you do?"
3. The conversation is going well and you want to keep in touch. What do you say?
A "Goodbye forever."
B "It was great talking to you. Let's stay in touch."
C "I have to go now."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at an international finance conference and you see someone standing alone. Break the ice and start a conversation. Include: a greeting, one social question, and one question to find common ground.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1 Hi, I do not think we have met. I am Eduarda. Oi, acho que não nos conhecemos. Eu sou a Eduarda.
2 What brings you to the conference? O que te traz à conferência?
3 Is this your first time at this event? É a sua primeira vez neste evento?
4 Have you been to São Paulo before? Você já esteve em São Paulo antes?
5 It was great talking to you. Let's stay in touch. Foi ótimo conversar com você. Vamos manter contato.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

Turn-Taking: Interrupting & Yielding Politely

Discourse markers + interruption phrases: get in, hold your turn, give the floor
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key turn-taking expressions for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Jump In -- to join a conversation quickly when you get the chance
"Sorry to jump in, but I have a quick point."
Chime In -- to add your comment to a discussion
"Feel free to chime in at any time."
Yield the Floor -- to stop speaking and let someone else talk
"I'll yield the floor to my colleague."
Go Ahead -- used to invite someone to speak or continue
"Please, go ahead. I would like to hear your view."
Hear Out -- to listen until someone finishes before you respond
"Let's hear her out before we react."
Talk Over -- to speak at the same time as someone else
"Sorry, I did not mean to talk over you."
Wrap Up -- to bring a point or a meeting to an end
"Let's wrap up this topic and move on."
Hang On -- to ask someone to wait a moment
"Hang on, let me finish my point."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each expression with its definition.

Jump In
Chime In
Yield the Floor
Go Ahead
Hear Out
Talk Over
Wrap Up
Hang On

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

In the weekly committee call, three people are speaking. David, the chair, asks for opinions. Anna starts to explain, but Eduarda has urgent news. "Sorry to jump in," she says, "but the creditors have a hard deadline." Anna replies, "Hang on, let me finish my point." Eduarda apologizes: "Of course, please go ahead. I did not mean to talk over you." Later, David invites Eduarda to chime in. She suggests they hear everyone out and then wrap up the timeline. Because everyone takes turns and uses polite discourse markers like "well", "anyway", and "as I was saying", the meeting stays calm and productive.

1. Why does Eduarda apologize to Anna?
A Because she is late.
B Because she talked over Anna before she finished.
C Because she disagrees with the deadline.
2. Which phrase invites someone to speak?
A "Hang on."
B "Please, go ahead."
C "Sorry to jump in."
3. What does Eduarda suggest at the end?
A To hear everyone out and then wrap up the timeline.
B To end the meeting immediately.
C To ignore the creditors.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Discourse markers = small words/phrases that signal your turn in a conversation (palavrinhas que sinalizam DE QUEM é a vez de falar). Each function has its own marker.

FunctionPhraseExample
InterruptSorry to jump in, but..."Sorry to jump in, but the deadline is close."
Hold the floorHang on, let me finish..."Hang on, let me finish my point."
YieldGo ahead / Please, you first"Please, go ahead."
ReturnAnyway, as I was saying..."Anyway, as I was saying, the timeline is tight."

Common Mistake:

"Stop! I want to talk." / "No, listen to me."

"Sorry to jump in, but..." / "If I could just add one thing..."

Tradução direta soa rude em inglês. Um marcador suave abre espaço sem ser agressivo.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each turn-taking phrase with the correct word.

"Sorry to in, but I have a quick point."
"Please, ahead."
" on, let me finish my point."
"Anyway, as I saying, the deadline is close."
"Let us her out before we react."
"I will the floor to my colleague."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the polite turn-taking exchange in the correct order. Listen first.

? "Of course, please go ahead."
? "Sorry to jump in, but I have a quick point about the deadline."
? "Good. Let us hear the team out and then wrap up."
? "Thank you. Anyway, as I was saying, we need an extension."
? "Hang on, let me finish my current point first."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Sorry to jump in, but I have a quick point.
Please, go ahead. I would like to hear your view.
Let us hear everyone out before we decide.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Two colleagues are talking and you have an urgent point. What is the most polite way to interrupt?
A "Stop, listen to me."
B "Sorry to jump in, but I have a quick point."
C "I talk now."
2. Someone interrupts you before you finish. How do you hold the floor politely?
A "Be quiet, it is my turn."
B "Hang on, let me finish my point."
C "You always talk."
3. You accidentally spoke at the same time as a colleague. What do you say?
A "Sorry, I did not mean to talk over you. Please, go ahead."
B "You started first."
C "It does not matter."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are in a busy meeting and two colleagues are talking over each other. You need to add an important point and then give the floor back. What do you say? Include: a polite interruption, your point, and an invitation for someone else to speak.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1 Sorry to jump in, but I have a quick point. Desculpa interromper, mas tenho um ponto rápido.
2 Hang on, let me finish my point. Espera, deixa eu terminar meu ponto.
3 Please, go ahead. Por favor, pode falar.
4 I did not mean to talk over you. Não quis falar por cima de você.
5 Let's wrap up and move on. Vamos encerrar e seguir em frente.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Defending a Position: Arguing with Data

First conditional + opinion frames: state, support and defend your view
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key argument expressions for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

In My View -- used to introduce your opinion
"In my view, the timing is right."
The Data Suggests -- used to present evidence
"The data suggests the market will recover."
Make a Case -- to argue in favor of something
"I would make a case for moving now."
Back Up -- to support a claim with evidence
"Can you back up that claim?"
Upside -- the positive part or potential benefit
"The upside is a higher return."
Downside -- the negative part or risk
"The downside is the market risk."
Bottom Line -- the most important point or conclusion
"The bottom line is we should act now."
Push Back -- to disagree or resist respectfully
"I want to push back on that estimate."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each expression with its definition.

In My View
The Data Suggests
Make a Case
Back Up
Upside
Downside
Bottom Line
Push Back

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

At the investment committee, Eduarda defends her recommendation. "In my view, we should move now," she begins. A skeptical partner asks her to back up the claim. "The data suggests the market will recover next quarter," she explains. She argues the consequence: "If we wait, we will lose the deal." The partner sees the upside but worries about the downside. Eduarda answers with another conditional: "If the market falls, our capital will stay protected." When a colleague proposes a lower estimate, she pushes back politely. Finally she gives the bottom line: "If we act now, we will lead the deal."

1. What does the partner ask Eduarda to do with her claim?
A To withdraw it.
B To back it up with evidence.
C To repeat it louder.
2. Which sentence is a first conditional?
A "In my view, we should move now."
B "If we wait, we will lose the deal."
C "The data suggests the market will recover."
3. What is Eduarda's bottom line?
A If they act now, they will lead the deal.
B They should cancel the deal.
C The market will never recover.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

First conditional = a real, possible future result. Structure: If + present simple, ... will + base verb (situação real e possível: a parte do IF fica no presente; o WILL só na consequência).

PartFormExample
If-clausepresent simple"If we wait,"
Resultwill + base verb"we will lose the deal."
Opinion frameIn my view, ..."In my view, we should act now."

Common Mistake:

"If we will wait, we will lose the deal."

"If we wait, we will lose the deal."

Nunca use "will" na parte do IF. O WILL fica só na consequência.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"If we , we will lose the deal."
"If the market improves, the company recover."
"In my , we should act now."
"Can you up that claim?"
"The suggests the market will recover."
"What is the ?"

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the argument in the correct order. Listen first.

? "If we wait, we will lose the deal."
? "In my view, we should act now."
? "The bottom line is: if we act now, we will lead the deal."
? "Of course, I can back up that claim with numbers."
? "The data suggests the market will recover next quarter."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

In my view, we should act now.
If we wait, we will lose the deal.
The data suggests the market will recover next quarter.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to state your opinion at the start of an argument. What do you say?
A "It is obvious."
B "In my view, we should act now."
C "Maybe, I do not know."
2. A partner doubts your claim. How do you support it?
A "Trust me."
B "The data suggests the market will recover. I can back it up with numbers."
C "It is just my feeling."
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "If we will act now, we lead the deal."
B "If we act now, we will lead the deal."
C "If we act now, we leading the deal."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are defending a real recommendation at work and a colleague disagrees. Make your case. Include: an opinion frame, one piece of evidence ("the data suggests..."), and a first conditional ("if we... we will...").

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1 In my view, we should act now. Na minha opinião, devemos agir agora.
2 The data suggests the market will recover. Os dados indicam que o mercado vai se recuperar.
3 Can you back up that claim? Você consegue embasar essa afirmação?
4 What is the downside? Qual é o risco / lado negativo?
5 If we wait, we will lose the deal. Se a gente esperar, vamos perder o negócio.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

Summarizing & Clarifying in Real Time

Reporting verbs + paraphrasing: sum up, report and check understanding
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key summary expressions for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Sum Up -- to give a short version of the main points
"Let me sum up the main points."
Recap -- to go over the main points again briefly
"Let me recap the decisions."
In Other Words -- used to say the same thing in a simpler way
"In other words, we act now but stay flexible."
Key Takeaway -- the most important point to remember
"The key takeaway is: act now and review later."
Follow -- to understand what someone means
"Do you follow me so far?"
On the Same Page -- to share the same understanding
"Are we all on the same page?"
Put Simply -- used to introduce a clear, simple version
"Put simply, we agreed to act now."
Action Item -- a task someone must do after the meeting
"Each person has one action item."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each expression with its definition.

Sum Up
Recap
In Other Words
Key Takeaway
Follow
On the Same Page
Put Simply
Action Item

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

At the end of the committee call, Eduarda closes the meeting. "Let me sum up the main points," she begins. She reports the decisions: "Daniel said that we will move now, and Anna suggested a short extension." A colleague does not follow, so Eduarda paraphrases: "In other words, we act now but stay flexible." She gives the key takeaway: act now, review in two weeks. Then she checks understanding: "Put simply, are we all on the same page?" Everyone agrees. Finally she assigns one action item to each person, and Clara offers to send a recap email.

1. What does Eduarda do at the end of the meeting?
A She starts a new topic.
B She sums up the main points and reports the decisions.
C She leaves without a summary.
2. Which reporting verb reports Daniel's words?
A "asked"
B "said that"
C "wanted"
3. Why does Eduarda paraphrase with "in other words"?
A Because a colleague did not follow her summary.
B Because she changed her mind.
C Because the meeting is starting.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Reporting verbs = report what someone said: reporting verb + (that) + clause. Atenção: tell precisa de pessoa ("tell me"), say não ("say that...").

VerbStructureExample
saysay + (that) + clause"He said that the deal is closed."
telltell + someone + (that) + clause"She told me that the data is strong."
suggestsuggest + (that) + clause"Anna suggested that we wait."

Common Mistake:

"He said me that the deal is closed."

"He told me that the deal is closed." / "He said that the deal is closed."

"tell" leva pessoa (tell me); "say" não (say that).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"Daniel that we will move now."
"Anna a short extension."
"In other , we keep flexibility."
"Let me up the main points."
"Just to confirm, are we all on the same ?"
"I will send a email to the team."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the meeting summary in the correct order. Listen first.

? "In other words, we act now but keep some flexibility."
? "Before we finish, let me sum up the main points."
? "I will send a recap email to the team."
? "The key takeaway is: act now and review in two weeks."
? "Daniel said that we will move now."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Let me sum up the main points.
She said that the market will recover.
Just to confirm, are we all on the same page?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. The meeting is ending and you want to start your summary. What do you say?
A "That is all, bye."
B "Before we finish, let me sum up the main points."
C "I forgot what we said."
2. You report what your manager said. Which sentence is correct?
A "She said me that the data is strong."
B "She told me that the data is strong."
C "She told that the data is strong."
3. A colleague looks confused after your summary. What do you say?
A "Put simply, we act now but stay flexible. Do you follow me?"
B "You should know already."
C "Never mind."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are closing a real meeting at work. Sum it up. Include: an opening ("Let me sum up..."), one reported sentence ("X said that..."), and a check ("Are we all on the same page?").

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1 Let me sum up the main points. Deixa eu resumir os pontos principais.
2 In other words, we act now but keep flexibility. Em outras palavras, agimos agora mas mantemos flexibilidade.
3 The key takeaway is: act now and review in two weeks. O ponto principal é: agir agora e revisar em duas semanas.
4 Just to confirm, are we all on the same page? Só pra confirmar, estamos todos alinhados?
5 I will send a recap email to the team. Vou mandar um e-mail de recap para a equipe.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

Review & Simulation: Strategy Meeting

Integration of lessons 6-9: ice-breaking, turn-taking, defending, summarizing
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Review

Vocabulary

These key expressions come from lessons 6-9. Tap Listen to review the pronunciation.

Break the Ice -- Lesson 6: to start a conversation in a relaxed way
"I always break the ice with a simple question."
Common Ground -- Lesson 6: a shared interest or experience
"We found common ground in finance."
Jump In -- Lesson 7: to join a conversation quickly
"Sorry to jump in, but I have a point."
Yield the Floor -- Lesson 7: to let someone else speak
"I'll yield the floor to my colleague."
In My View -- Lesson 8: used to introduce your opinion
"In my view, the timing is right."
Back Up -- Lesson 8: to support a claim with evidence
"Can you back up that claim?"
Sum Up -- Lesson 9: to give a short version of the main points
"Let me sum up the main points."
On the Same Page -- Lesson 9: to share the same understanding
"Are we all on the same page?"

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each expression with its definition.

Break the Ice
Common Ground
Jump In
Yield the Floor
In My View
Back Up
Sum Up
On the Same Page

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

At the strategy meeting, Eduarda uses everything from this block. She opens by breaking the ice: "Nice to see you all again." She gives her opinion: "In my view, we should act now," and backs it up: "The data suggests the market will recover." She argues a consequence with a first conditional: "If we wait, we will lose the deal." When David jumps in to ask about the risk, she answers and then yields the floor: "Anna, please go ahead." Finally she reports and sums up: "Daniel said that we will move now. To sum up: act now, review in two weeks." Everyone is on the same page.

1. Which skill does Eduarda use to OPEN the meeting?
A Reporting verbs.
B Breaking the ice.
C Summarizing.
2. Which sentence is a first conditional?
A "In my view, we should act now."
B "If we wait, we will lose the deal."
C "Nice to see you all again."
3. How does she report what Daniel said?
A "Daniel said that we will move now."
B "Daniel said me to move now."
C "Daniel told that move now."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

The four structures of the block, in the order of a meeting (os quatro skills, na ordem de uma reunião):

SkillStructureExample
Question form (A6)Wh-/aux + subject + verb?"What do you do?"
Turn-taking (A7)Sorry to jump in, but... / Go ahead"Sorry to jump in, but..."
First conditional (A8)If + present, ... will + base"If we wait, we will lose."
Reporting verb (A9)said / told someone (that) + clause"He told me that..."

Top mistakes of the block:

"If we will wait..." / "He said me that..."

"If we wait..." / "He told me that..."

No IF use present simple (sem "will"); use "tell" com pessoa, "say" sem pessoa.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Each sentence reviews a different skill. Complete it with the correct word.

" brings you to the summit?"
"Sorry to in, but I have a quick point."
"If we , we will lose the deal."
"Daniel that we will move now."
"Let me up the main points."
"Are we all on the same ?"

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put Eduarda's pitch in the correct order. Listen first.

? "If we wait, we will lose the deal."
? "Nice to see you all again. In my view, we should act now."
? "Are we all on the same page?"
? "To sum up: act now, review in two weeks."
? "The data suggests the market will recover."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Sorry to jump in, but I have a quick point.
In my view, if we wait, we will lose the deal.
Daniel said that we will move now.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You open a meeting and want to break the ice. What do you say?
A "Let's start, no time to waste."
B "Nice to see you all again. How was everyone's week?"
C "Who are you all?"
2. You defend your position. Which sentence is correct?
A "In my view, if we will wait, we lose the deal."
B "In my view, if we wait, we will lose the deal."
C "In my view, if we wait, we losing the deal."
3. You close the meeting. What do you say?
A "To sum up: act now, review in two weeks. Are we all on the same page?"
B "That's it, goodbye."
C "I forgot the decisions."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you run a real strategy meeting from start to finish. In one recording: break the ice, give an opinion with a first conditional, report what a colleague said, and sum up with a confirmation.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10 (Block 2)

1 Nice to see you all again. What brings you here? Que bom ver vocês de novo. O que traz você aqui?
2 Sorry to jump in, but I have a quick point. Desculpa interromper, mas tenho um ponto rápido.
3 In my view, if we wait, we will lose the deal. Na minha opinião, se a gente esperar, vamos perder o negócio.
4 Daniel said that we will move now. O Daniel disse que vamos agir agora.
5 To sum up: act now, review in two weeks. On the same page? Resumindo: agir agora, revisar em duas semanas. Estamos alinhados?
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Chapter 11 -- Legal Framework Vocabulary

Legal collocations + passive voice: describe a restructuring case in English
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key Chapter 11 terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

File for Bankruptcy -- to officially begin a legal bankruptcy process
"The company decided to file for bankruptcy."
Automatic Stay -- a court order that stops creditors from collecting
"The automatic stay protects the company."
Debtor in Possession -- a company that keeps control of its assets in Chapter 11
"As debtor in possession, the firm keeps running."
Reorganization Plan -- a formal plan to restructure debts and operations
"The reorganization plan was filed with the court."
Discharge -- to legally release a debtor from certain debts
"Some debts are discharged at the end of the case."
Insolvent -- unable to pay debts when they are due
"The company became insolvent last year."
Creditors' Committee -- a group that represents the creditors' interests
"The creditors' committee reviewed the plan."
Court Approval -- official permission from the court
"The sale needs court approval."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

File for Bankruptcy
Automatic Stay
Debtor in Possession
Reorganization Plan
Discharge
Insolvent
Creditors' Committee
Court Approval

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Vertex Industries became insolvent and decided to file for bankruptcy. A petition is filed with the court, and immediately an automatic stay is granted, so all collections are stopped. The company is protected while it keeps control as debtor in possession. A reorganization plan is prepared and, once it is accepted by the creditors' committee, it must be approved by the court. At the end, some debts are discharged.

1. What happens immediately after the petition is filed?
A The debts are discharged.
B An automatic stay is granted and collections are stopped.
C The company is sold.
2. Why is the passive used ("is filed", "are stopped")?
A Because the action is in the future.
B Because the process matters more than who does it.
C Because it is a question.
3. Who must approve the reorganization plan?
A The court must approve it.
B The creditors discharge it.
C No one needs to approve it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

The passive voice = be + past participle. Use it when the action matters more than who does it (muito comum em inglês jurídico). Adicione by + agente só quando o agente for relevante.

TenseStructureExample
Presentis / are + past participle"A petition is filed with the court."
Pastwas / were + past participle"The plan was filed last week."
Modalmodal + be + past participle"It must be approved by the court."

Common Mistake:

"The petition filed yesterday by the lawyer."

"The petition was filed yesterday by the lawyer."

A passiva sempre precisa de be (is/are/was/were) + particípio. Nunca omita o "be".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each passive sentence with the correct word.

"A petition is with the court."
"Creditors are of the automatic stay."
"The plan must be by the court."
"The company is from creditors."
"Some debts are at the end of the case."
"The company decided to file bankruptcy."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the Chapter 11 process in the correct order. Listen first.

? "The company keeps control as debtor in possession."
? "A petition is filed with the court."
? "The plan is approved by the court."
? "A reorganization plan is prepared and filed."
? "An automatic stay is granted to stop collections."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

The petition was filed with the court.
Creditors are notified of the automatic stay.
The reorganization plan must be approved by the court.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A client asks how the case officially begins. What do you say?
A "We just stop paying everyone."
B "A petition is filed with the court."
C "The court files our company."
2. You explain the obligation about the plan. Which sentence is correct?
A "The plan must approved by the court."
B "The plan must be approved by the court."
C "The plan must to be approve by the court."
3. The client worries about creditors. How do you reassure them?
A "The company is protected by an automatic stay, so collections are stopped."
B "The creditors take everything immediately."
C "There is nothing we can do."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are briefing a colleague on a Chapter 11 case. Explain it in 3-4 sentences. Use at least two key terms and the passive voice ("A petition is filed...", "The plan must be approved...").

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1 The petition was filed with the court. A petição foi protocolada no tribunal.
2 The company is protected by an automatic stay. A empresa é protegida por uma suspensão automática.
3 The reorganization plan must be approved by the court. O plano de reorganização precisa ser aprovado pelo tribunal.
4 As debtor in possession, the company keeps control. Como devedora na posse, a empresa mantém o controle.
5 Some debts are discharged at the end of the case. Algumas dívidas são quitadas ao final do caso.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Creditor Negotiations -- Language of Compromise

Negotiation collocations + modal verbs (could/would/might): propose, concede, and close a deal in English
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key negotiation terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Counteroffer -- a new offer made in response to one you reject
"They rejected our bid and sent a counteroffer."
Make a Concession -- to give something up to move a deal forward
"We made a concession on the interest rate."
Sticking Point -- an issue that blocks an agreement
"The interest rate was the main sticking point."
Settlement -- a final agreement that ends a dispute
"Both sides reached a settlement."
Debt Haircut -- a reduction in the amount a creditor is owed
"Creditors accepted a thirty percent debt haircut."
Standstill Agreement -- a deal to pause all action for a set period
"The banks signed a standstill agreement."
Good Faith -- an honest intention to deal fairly
"We negotiated in good faith."
Walk Away -- to leave a negotiation without a deal
"If they refuse, we are ready to walk away."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Counteroffer
Make a Concession
Sticking Point
Settlement
Debt Haircut
Standstill Agreement
Good Faith
Walk Away

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Vertex Industries had to negotiate with its creditors. Our team opened with a proposal: "We could offer a longer repayment period." The creditors sent a counteroffer and asked, "Would you accept a smaller discount?" We decided to make a concession on the interest rate to find common ground. After a short standstill agreement, both sides felt a settlement might be possible. We negotiated in good faith, and our strong cash position gave us leverage.

1. How did our team open the negotiation?
A With a direct order: "Accept our terms."
B With a polite proposal using "could".
C By refusing to talk.
2. Why are "could" and "would" used here?
A Because the actions are in the past.
B Because they make proposals and requests sound polite and tentative.
C Because they are questions.
3. What gave our team an advantage in the negotiation?
A Their strong cash position (leverage).
B They refused every counteroffer.
C They had no money at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use could / would / might to sound polite and tentative in a negotiation. Eles deixam a proposta menos agressiva e mais profissional. Sempre vêm seguidos do verbo base (sem "to", sem -s, sem -ed).

ModalUseExample
couldsoft proposal / suggestion"We could offer a longer timeline."
wouldpolite request / hypothetical"Would you accept a haircut?"
mighttentative possibility"That might work for us."

Common Mistake:

"We could to offer a longer timeline."

"We could offer a longer timeline."

Modais (could/would/might) são sempre seguidos do verbo base. Nunca use "to" depois do modal.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each negotiation sentence with the correct word.

"We offer a longer repayment period."
" you be willing to accept a debt haircut?"
"That work for us."
"We could halfway on the rate."
"They might not the first offer."
"Could we a standstill agreement?"

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the negotiation process in the correct order. Listen first.

? "They explore possible trade-offs and concessions."
? "Both sides present their opening positions."
? "Both sides agree on a win-win settlement."
? "A counteroffer is made to meet halfway."
? "Each side identifies its main sticking point."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

We could offer a longer repayment period.
Would you be willing to accept a debt haircut?
That might work for us, if we meet halfway.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to propose extending the deadline politely. What do you say?
A "You must give us more time."
B "We could extend the deadline by thirty days."
C "Give us a longer deadline now."
2. Which sentence is grammatically correct?
A "We could to offer a discount."
B "We could offer a discount."
C "We could offered a discount."
3. The other side rejects your offer and sends a new one. What do you call it?
A "A counteroffer."
B "A discharge."
C "A petition."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are negotiating with a creditor. In 3-4 sentences, make an opening proposal and a polite request. Use at least two key terms and the modals could, would, or might ("We could offer...", "Would you be willing to...").

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1 We could offer a longer repayment period. Poderíamos oferecer um prazo de pagamento mais longo.
2 Would you be willing to accept a debt haircut? Você estaria disposto a aceitar uma redução da dívida?
3 That might work for us. Isso talvez funcione para nós.
4 We could meet halfway and find common ground. Poderíamos ceder de ambos os lados e achar um meio-termo.
5 I think we have a settlement we can both accept. Acho que temos um acordo que ambos podemos aceitar.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Closing the Deal -- Signing & Contract Terms

Signing and contract vocabulary + first conditional (if/unless + will): state conditions and close a deal in English
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key signing and contract terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Execute -- to sign a contract and make it legally valid
"Both parties will execute the agreement on Friday."
Binding -- legally obligating both sides to follow the deal
"Once signed, the contract is binding."
Terms and Conditions -- the rules and details written in an agreement
"The legal team reviewed the terms and conditions."
Default -- to fail to pay a debt or meet an obligation
"If the company defaults, the lender can act."
Waive -- to give up a right or claim on purpose
"The lender agreed to waive the late fee."
Breach -- a failure to follow the terms of a contract
"Missing the deadline would be a breach of contract."
In Writing -- recorded formally on paper, not just spoken
"Any change must be confirmed in writing."
Enforceable -- able to be required and upheld by law
"A signed contract is fully enforceable."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Execute
Binding
Terms and Conditions
Default
Waive
Breach
In Writing
Enforceable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

The Vertex restructuring was ready to close. Our team sent the final draft, and the creditors reviewed the terms and conditions. We told them: "If both parties sign, the contract will be binding." They asked one question: "If the company defaults, what happens?" We promised a cure period, confirmed in writing. "Unless that period is met, it counts as a breach," we explained. Both sides agreed not to waive any clause without written consent. Once they execute the agreement, it becomes fully enforceable.

1. When does the contract become binding?
A As soon as the draft is sent.
B If both parties sign it.
C Only after a breach.
2. Why are "if" and "unless" used here?
A Because the actions already happened.
B To link a real condition to its result (first conditional).
C Because they are imaginary, impossible situations.
3. How can a clause be waived?
A Only with written consent from both sides.
B By one side alone, without telling the other.
C It can never be waived.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use the first conditional to link a real, possible condition to its result. Use if / unless + present simple in the condition, and will + base verb in the result. Usamos isso para condições reais e prováveis (não imaginárias). Unless significa "if not" (se não).

PartFormExample
Conditionif / unless + present simple"If both parties sign..."
Resultwill + base verb"...the contract will be binding."
unless= if not"Unless they pay = If they do not pay"

Common Mistake:

"If they will sign, the deal will close."

"If they sign, the deal will close."

Depois de if ou unless, use o presente simples. Nunca use "will" na parte da condição — só no resultado.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"If both parties sign, the contract be binding."
"If the company , the lender will act."
" we confirm in writing, the change will not count."
"The deal will close on Friday if they today."
"If they breach the terms, we will not the penalty."
"Once both sides the contract, it is enforceable."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the closing process in the correct order. Listen first.

? "Each side confirms any changes in writing."
? "Both parties agree on the final terms and conditions."
? "The agreement becomes enforceable on the closing date."
? "The signatories execute the contract."
? "The lawyers prepare a binding draft for review."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

If your team approves, we will sign today.
If the company defaults, the lender will act.
Unless we confirm in writing, the change will not count.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to state the condition for signing today. What do you say?
A "If your team will approve, we sign today."
B "If your team approves, we will sign today."
C "If your team approve, we will signing today."
2. Which sentence is grammatically correct?
A "Unless they will pay, we will act."
B "Unless they pay, we will act."
C "Unless they pay, we acting."
3. Someone breaks a term of the signed contract. What is that called?
A "A breach."
B "A waiver."
C "A signature."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are closing a deal with a creditor. In 3-4 sentences, present the terms and state two conditions using the first conditional. Use at least two key terms ("If both parties sign...", "Unless... we will...").

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1 If your team approves, we will sign today. Se a sua equipe aprovar, nós assinaremos hoje.
2 Once signed, the contract will be binding. Uma vez assinado, o contrato será vinculante.
3 If the company defaults, the lender will act. Se a empresa não pagar, o credor agirá.
4 Unless we confirm in writing, the change will not count. A menos que confirmemos por escrito, a alteração não valerá.
5 If both parties execute it, the contract will be enforceable. Se ambas as partes o assinarem, o contrato será exequível.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Post-Closing -- Monitoring & Compliance

Monitoring and compliance vocabulary + present perfect continuous (have/has been + -ing): report ongoing actions and give a status update in English
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key monitoring and compliance terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Covenant -- a formal promise written into a loan agreement
"The borrower must respect every financial covenant."
Compliance -- following the rules and terms of an agreement
"We check the borrower's compliance every quarter."
Monitor -- to watch and track something over time
"Our team monitors the debt levels closely."
Milestone -- a key target or checkpoint to reach by a date
"The borrower has hit every milestone so far."
Flag -- to point out a problem so others notice it
"The analyst flagged a late report this week."
Escalate -- to raise an issue to a higher level for action
"We escalated the problem to the credit committee."
Remediation -- the action of fixing a problem or breach
"They agreed on a remediation plan for the issue."
Track Record -- a history of past results and performance
"The borrower has a solid track record so far."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Covenant
Compliance
Monitor
Milestone
Flag
Escalate
Remediation
Track Record

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

A month after the Vertex deal closed, our monitoring team got to work. We have been monitoring all the financial covenants since the closing, and the borrower has been complying with every milestone. Last week, an analyst flagged one item: the reporting package arrived late. We have been following up with their finance team, so I have already escalated it to the credit committee. A remediation plan is now in place. Overall, their track record has been solid.

1. How long has the team been monitoring the covenants?
A For one week only.
B Since the closing.
C They have not started yet.
2. Why is "have been monitoring" used instead of "monitor"?
A Because the action is finished and over.
B To show an action that started in the past and is still going on.
C Because it describes a future plan.
3. What has already been done with the flagged item?
A It has been escalated to the credit committee.
B It has been ignored.
C The deal has been cancelled.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use the present perfect continuous to report an action that started in the past and is still going on (or stopped very recently). Form: have / has been + verb-ing. Usamos isso para acoes em andamento desde um ponto no passado ate agora. Use since + ponto no tempo (since the closing) e for + periodo (for three weeks).

PartFormExample
Ongoing actionhave / has been + verb-ing"We have been monitoring..."
Start pointsince + point in time"...since the closing."
Periodfor + period"...for three weeks."

Common Mistake:

"We monitor the covenants since the closing."

"We have been monitoring the covenants since the closing."

Para uma acao que comecou no passado e continua ate agora, use have/has been + verbo-ing — nunca o presente simples.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"We have been the covenants since the closing."
"The borrower has been with every milestone."
"We have been following up with their finance team two days."
"How long you been tracking the debt levels?"
"I have already it to the credit committee."
"Their track record has been solid the closing."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the monitoring process in the correct order. Listen first.

? "An analyst has flagged one item that needs attention."
? "The monitoring team has been tracking the covenants since the closing."
? "A remediation plan has been agreed and put in place."
? "The team has escalated the issue to the credit committee."
? "They have been collecting the borrower's quarterly reports."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

We have been monitoring the covenants since the closing.
The borrower has been complying with every milestone.
I have already escalated it to the credit committee.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to report that monitoring started at closing and continues now. What do you say?
A "We monitor the covenants since the closing."
B "We have been monitoring the covenants since the closing."
C "We are monitoring the covenants for the closing."
2. Which sentence is grammatically correct?
A "How long you have been tracking it?"
B "How long have you been tracking it?"
C "How long have you been track it?"
