3. You raise an issue to a higher level so the committee can act. What is that called?
A "To escalate it."
B "To waive it."
C "To execute it."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are giving a status update on a deal you have been watching since it closed. In 3-4 sentences, report what you have been monitoring and how compliance is going, using the present perfect continuous ("We have been monitoring...", "They have been complying..."). Use at least two key terms.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1 We have been monitoring the covenants since the closing. Estamos monitorando os covenants desde o fechamento.
2 The borrower has been complying with every milestone. O tomador vem cumprindo todos os marcos.
3 I have been following up with their finance team for two days. Venho cobrando a equipe financeira deles há dois dias.
4 I have already escalated it to the credit committee. Já escalei isso para o comitê de crédito.
5 Their track record has been solid since the closing. O histórico deles tem sido sólido desde o fechamento.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Restructuring Outcomes -- Lessons Learned

Review and lessons-learned vocabulary + third conditional (if + past perfect, would have): talk about hindsight and run a post-mortem in English
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key review and lessons-learned terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Post-Mortem -- a review held after a project or deal is finished
"We held a post-mortem the week after the deal closed."
Outcome -- the final result of a process or deal
"The outcome of the restructuring was positive overall."
Root Cause -- the deepest underlying reason for a problem
"The root cause of the delay was a late report."
Hindsight -- understanding something only after it has happened
"In hindsight, we should have acted faster."
Overlook -- to fail to notice or consider something important
"We overlooked an early warning sign in the reports."
Recur -- to happen again, often more than once
"We updated the checklist so the issue will not recur."
Downturn -- a period when the economy or market gets weaker
"A sharp downturn could have changed the outcome."
Debrief -- a meeting to review what happened after an event
"Let us walk through the deal in the debrief."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Post-Mortem
Outcome
Root Cause
Hindsight
Overlook
Recur
Downturn
Debrief

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

At the Vertex post-mortem, the team reviewed the outcome of the deal. It was positive, but there was one lesson. The root cause of the delay was an early reporting gap that we overlooked. In hindsight, if we had caught it sooner, we would have avoided two weeks of follow-up. If the downturn had been deeper, the outcome could have been very different. We have updated the checklist, so the issue will not recur. The full takeaways go into the debrief notes.

1. Did the team actually catch the gap early?
A Yes, they caught it on day one.
B No -- "if we had caught it" describes an imagined past.
C The text does not say.
2. Why is "would have avoided" used?
A Because it is a real plan for the future.
B To describe an imagined result of a past that did not happen.
C Because the action is still going on now.
3. What has been done so the issue will not recur?
A The checklist has been updated.
B The deal has been cancelled.
C Nothing has been done.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use the third conditional to imagine a different past — one that did not happen. Form: If + had + past participle (the imagined condition), would / could have + past participle (the imagined result). Usamos isso para falar de hindsight: o que TERIA acontecido. O passado nao pode mudar. Compare com a Aula 13 (first conditional, futuro real): aqui e um passado imaginario.

PartFormExample
Imagined conditionIf + had + past participle"If we had caught it sooner..."
Imagined resultwould have + past participle"...we would have avoided the delay."
Softer resultcould have + past participle"...it could have been worse."

Common Mistake:

"If we would have caught it sooner, we would have avoided the delay."

"If we had caught it sooner, we would have avoided the delay."

A if-clause leva had + participio — nunca "would have". So o resultado usa would/could have.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"If we caught it sooner, we would have avoided the delay."
"If they had escalated earlier, we would not lost two weeks."
"The of the delay was a late report."
"In , we should have acted faster."
"We have updated the checklist, so the issue will not ."
"What would you done differently?"

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the lessons-learned process in the correct order. Listen first.

? "They realized they had overlooked an early reporting gap."
? "The team held a debrief after the deal closed."
? "They wrote up the lesson learned so the problem would not recur."
? "In hindsight, they agreed they would have acted faster."
? "They looked for the root cause of the delay."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

In hindsight, if we had caught it sooner, we would have avoided the delay.
The root cause was a gap we overlooked early on.
We have updated the checklist, so the issue will not recur.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to imagine a different past: catching the gap sooner. What do you say?
A "If we would have caught it sooner, we avoided the delay."
B "If we had caught it sooner, we would have avoided the delay."
C "If we catch it sooner, we will avoid the delay."
2. Which sentence is grammatically correct?
A "What would you done differently?"
B "What would you have done differently?"
C "What you would have done differently?"
3. You name the deepest underlying reason for the problem. What is that called?
A "The root cause."
B "The downturn."
C "The outcome."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Think of a deal or project that has finished. In 3-4 sentences, run a mini post-mortem: name the outcome and the root cause, and use the third conditional to say what would have been different in hindsight ("If we had..., we would have..."). Use at least two key terms.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1 Overall, the outcome was positive, but there is one clear lesson. No geral, o resultado foi positivo, mas há uma lição clara.
2 The root cause was a gap we overlooked early on. A causa raiz foi uma falha que deixamos passar no começo.
3 In hindsight, if we had caught it sooner, we would have avoided the delay. Em retrospecto, se tivéssemos notado antes, teríamos evitado o atraso.
4 If the downturn had been deeper, the outcome could have been worse. Se a queda tivesse sido mais forte, o resultado poderia ter sido pior.
5 We have updated the checklist, so the issue will not recur. Atualizamos o checklist, então o problema não vai se repetir.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Professional Emails -- Structure & Tone

Email and tone vocabulary + the formal register (I am writing to, could you please, please find attached): structure a clear, polite client email
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key email and tone terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Recipient -- the person who receives your message
"Always check the recipient before you hit send."
Salutation -- the opening greeting line of an email
"Use a formal salutation: Dear Mr. Carter."
Concise -- short and clear, with no extra words
"Keep the body concise: three short paragraphs."
Acknowledge -- to confirm that you received or read something
"Acknowledge their email before you make a request."
Attach -- to add a file to an email
"Please find the report attached."
Forward -- to send a received email on to another person
"I will forward you the client's reply."
Prompt -- quick and without delay
"Thank you for your prompt reply."
Sign-Off -- the closing line of an email (Best regards, ...)
"End with a warm sign-off, not just thanks."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Recipient
Salutation
Concise
Acknowledge
Attach
Forward
Prompt
Sign-Off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda starts her email with a formal salutation. Before any request, she acknowledges the client's last message. Then she states her purpose: "I am writing to confirm the timeline." She keeps the body concise. Instead of "send the numbers", she softens the request: "Could you please send the figures by Friday?" and "I would appreciate the report by end of day." She names the file: "Please find attached the deck", so the recipient does not miss it. She closes with a warm sign-off and thanks the client for a prompt reply.

1. Why does Eduarda write "Could you please send the figures?" instead of "Send the figures"?
A Because the formal version is faster to type.
B To soften the request and sound polite to the client.
C Because it is grammatically required for any request.
2. What does she do before making a request?
A She acknowledges the client's last message.
B She forwards the email to her director.
C She deletes the salutation.
3. How does she point the recipient to the file?
A "Here is the deck."
B "Please find attached the deck."
C "You can search for the deck."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use the formal register to make an email polite and professional. A direct command can sound rude to a client; a softened request gives them a choice. O registro formal suaviza o pedido: em vez de "Send it", escreva "Could you please send it?". Use formulas fixas para abrir, pedir e apontar anexos.

FunctionFormal formulaExample
State purposeI am writing to + verb"I am writing to confirm the timeline."
Soft requestCould you please + verb"Could you please send the figures?"
Point to a filePlease find attached + noun"Please find attached the deck."

Common Mistake:

"Give me the numbers by Friday."

"Could you please send the figures by Friday?"

Para um cliente, um pedido precisa de um suavizador ("could you please" / "I would appreciate") — nunca uma ordem seca.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"I am to confirm the timeline for the deal."
"Could you send the figures by Friday?"
"Please find the latest deck."
"Before I ask, let me your last message."
"Thank you for your reply this morning."
"End with a warm , not just thanks."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the steps of writing the email in the correct order. Listen first.

? "She kept the body concise and added the report as an attachment."
? "She opened the email by acknowledging the client's last message."
? "She signed off politely and sent it to the recipient."
? "She copied her director on the reply."
? "She stated her purpose in one clear line."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

I am writing to confirm the timeline for the deal.
Could you please send the figures by Friday?
Please find attached the updated deck.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You need the figures from a client. Which line is the most professional?
A "Send me the figures now."
B "Could you please send the figures by Friday?"
C "I need figures, fast."
2. You want to point the recipient to a file. What do you write?
A "The file is somewhere here."
B "Please find attached the report."
C "Go and look for the report."
3. You open an email to a client. What comes first?
A A formal salutation, then acknowledge their message.
B The request, with no greeting.
C The sign-off.

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Think of a real email you need to send a client or colleague. In 3-4 sentences, dictate it: open with a salutation, acknowledge their message, state your purpose, and make one softened request ("Could you please..." or "I would appreciate..."). Use at least two key terms.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1 Thank you for your message; I am writing to confirm the timeline. Obrigada pela sua mensagem; escrevo para confirmar o cronograma.
2 Could you please send the figures by Friday? Você poderia, por favor, enviar os números até sexta-feira?
3 I would appreciate the report by end of day. Eu agradeceria o relatório até o fim do dia.
4 Please find attached the updated deck. Segue em anexo a apresentação atualizada.
5 I look forward to your prompt reply. Best regards. Aguardo seu retorno breve. Atenciosamente.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Deal Memos -- Concise Written Communication

Memo and concision vocabulary + participle phrases (the deal closing Friday, the terms agreed last week): structure a tight, one-page deal memo
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key memo and concision terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Memo -- a short internal document that informs the team
"Send the deal memo to the partners by noon."
Headline -- the opening line that states the main message
"Lead with the headline: we recommend proceeding."
Rationale -- the reason behind a decision or recommendation
"Give the rationale in one clear sentence."
Scope -- the limit of what a deal or task includes
"Define the scope so no one assumes more."
Bullet Point -- a short item in a list, marked by a dot
"Use bullet points for the key terms, not paragraphs."
Draft -- a working version, before the final one
"Send me the draft before it goes out."
Circulate -- to send a document to a group of people
"Circulate the draft for review before it reaches the partners."
Outline -- the structure or skeleton before you write
"Start with an outline: headline, terms, rationale, scope."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Memo
Headline
Rationale
Scope
Bullet Point
Draft
Circulate
Outline

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda writes a deal memo. She opens with a headline that states the recommendation up front. She lists the key terms as bullet points, not paragraphs. To keep it tight, she cuts every long clause: instead of "the terms that were agreed last week", she writes "the terms agreed last week"; instead of "the deal that is closing on Friday", she writes "the deal closing on Friday". She gives the rationale in one line and defines the scope. Then she sends the draft to her director and will circulate the final version. She always starts from an outline.

1. Why does Eduarda write "the terms agreed last week" instead of "the terms that were agreed last week"?
A Because "that were" is grammatically wrong.
B To cut words and keep the memo tight, same meaning.
C Because the meaning changes to the future.
2. What does she put in the headline?
A The recommendation, up front.
B The full list of every detail.
C The sign-off.
3. How does she present the key terms?
A As long paragraphs.
B As bullet points.
C As a footnote.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use participle phrases to make writing concise. Cut "that is / that are / that was / that were / who is" and keep only the participle. A -ing form (closing, leading) shows an action in progress; a past participle (agreed, shown, sent) shows an action received. A participle phrase corta a relativa e mantem o sentido: em vez de "the deal that is closing", escreva "the deal closing".

Cut thisKeep (participle)Example
that is + -ingjust the -ing verb"the deal closing on Friday"
that were + past participlejust the past participle"the terms agreed last week"
who is + -ingjust the -ing verb"the partner leading the deal"

Common Mistake:

"the deal that is closing on Friday and the terms that were agreed last week"

"the deal closing on Friday and the terms agreed last week"

Num memo, cada "that is" ou "that was" extra e peso morto — corte e deixe so o participio.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"Lead with the : we recommend proceeding."
"Use points for the key terms."
"Give the in one clear sentence."
"Define the so no one assumes more."
"I will the draft for review."
"the terms last week" (from: the terms that were agreed)

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the steps of writing the memo in the correct order. Listen first.

? "She gave the rationale in one clear line."
? "She opened the memo with a headline stating the recommendation."
? "She circulated the draft to her director for review."
? "She defined the scope of the deal."
? "She listed the key terms as bullet points."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Headline: we recommend proceeding with the deal.
The deal closing on Friday is on track.
I will circulate the draft for review.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You open a deal memo for busy partners. What comes first?
A A long paragraph of background.
B A headline with the recommendation.
C The sign-off.
2. Which is the tighter, more professional phrase for a memo?
A "the deal that is closing on Friday"
B "the deal closing on Friday"
C "the deal which it is closing on Friday"
3. You want to list five key terms. How do you present them?
A As bullet points.
B In one long sentence.
C In a separate email each.

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Think of a real update you need to send your team. In 3-4 sentences, dictate a tight memo: open with a headline (the recommendation), give two key terms as bullet points, and add one rationale line. Cut at least one long clause into a participle phrase. Use at least two key terms.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1 Headline: we recommend proceeding with the deal. Destaque: recomendamos prosseguir com o negócio.
2 The deal closing on Friday is on track. O negócio que fecha na sexta está dentro do prazo.
3 Use bullet points for the key terms, not paragraphs. Use tópicos (bullet points) para os termos-chave, não parágrafos.
4 Rationale: the returns exceed our threshold. Justificativa: os retornos superam nosso patamar mínimo.
5 I will circulate the draft for review. Vou circular o rascunho para revisão.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Meeting Minutes -- Recording Decisions & Actions

Meeting-minutes vocabulary + reported speech backshift (He said the deal was on track; She said they would close): structure clear minutes
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key meeting-minutes terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Minutes -- the official written record of a meeting
"I will write up the minutes after the call."
Attendee -- a person who is present at a meeting
"List every attendee at the top of the minutes."
Apologies -- a record of who was absent but sent word
"Apologies were received from two directors."
Quorum -- the minimum number present needed to decide
"There was a quorum, so the vote was valid."
Resolution -- a formal decision recorded in the minutes
"The board passed a resolution to proceed."
Owner -- the person responsible for an action item
"Give each action an owner and a deadline."
Tabled -- postponed to a later meeting
"The pricing item was tabled until next week."
Adjourn -- to formally close or suspend the meeting
"The chair adjourned the meeting at noon."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Minutes
Attendee
Apologies
Quorum
Resolution
Owner
Tabled
Adjourn

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda writes the minutes of the board call. She lists the attendees and the apologies from those absent, and confirms there was a quorum. Because minutes are written after the meeting, she reports everything in the past. The chair said the deal was on track (not "is"); the CFO said they would close on Friday (not "will"); a director said they could proceed (not "can"). She records each decision as a resolution, gives every action an owner, marks the unresolved item as tabled, and notes that the chair adjourned the meeting.

1. Why does Eduarda write "the chair said the deal was on track" instead of "is on track"?
A Because the deal failed.
B Minutes are written after the meeting, so the tense backshifts to the past.
C Because "is" is never used in business English.
2. How does she report "we will close on Friday"?
A "She said they would close on Friday."
B "She said they will close on Friday."
C "She says they close Friday."
3. What does she do with an item that was not resolved?
A She deletes it.
B She marks it as tabled.
C She records it as a quorum.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use reported speech with backshift for minutes. Because the meeting is over, every quote moves one step into the past: is/are → was/were, will → would, can → could, present simple → past simple. Use said + (that). Como as minutes sao escritas depois da reuniao, o tempo verbal recua um passo: "is" vira "was", "will" vira "would", "can" vira "could".

Live (direct)Minute (reported)Example
is / arewas / were"He said the deal was on track."
willwould"She said they would close on Friday."
cancould"They said they could proceed."

Common Mistake:

"He says the deal is on track and we will close on Friday."

"He said the deal was on track and they would close on Friday."

As minutes registram o que ja foi dito — tudo recua um passo: is → was, will → would, can → could.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"He said the deal on track."
"She said they close on Friday."
"They said they proceed."
"The board passed a to proceed."
"The pricing item was until next week."
"The chair the meeting at noon."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the steps of writing the minutes in the correct order. Listen first.

? "She recorded each decision as a resolution with an owner."
? "She listed the attendees and noted the apologies at the top."
? "She wrote that the chair adjourned the meeting."
? "She marked the unresolved item as tabled."
? "She confirmed there was a quorum."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

He said the deal was on track.
The board passed a resolution to proceed.
The chair adjourned the meeting at noon.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. In the meeting, the partner said "The deal is on track." How do you write it in the minutes?
A "He says the deal is on track."
B "He said the deal was on track."
C "He said the deal is on track."
2. An item could not be decided today. How do you record it?
A Mark it as tabled until the next meeting.
B Leave it out of the minutes.
C Record it as a quorum.
3. You open the minutes. What goes at the top?
A The attendees and the apologies.
B The adjournment.
C The sign-off.

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Think of a real meeting you attended. In 3-4 sentences, dictate the minutes: name the attendees, report one thing someone said (in the past, with backshift), and record one decision as a resolution with an owner. Use at least two key terms.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1 The attendees were five directors; apologies from two. Os presentes eram cinco diretores; ausências justificadas de dois.
2 He said the deal was on track. Ele disse que o negócio estava dentro do previsto.
3 The board passed a resolution to proceed. O conselho aprovou uma resolução para prosseguir.
4 The pricing item was tabled until next week. O item de precificação foi adiado para a próxima semana.
5 The chair adjourned the meeting at noon. O presidente encerrou a reunião ao meio-dia.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Status Reports -- Updating Stakeholders in Writing

Status-report vocabulary + present perfect for results (We have completed due diligence; so far; not yet): structure a clear weekly update
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key status-report terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Status -- where something stands right now (on track / at risk)
"Headline status: the deal is on track."
Update -- the new information since the last report
"Here is the weekly update for the stakeholders."
Progress -- the advance already made toward a goal
"We have made strong progress this week."
Pending -- still waiting, not yet finished or approved
"The lender approval is still pending."
Blocker -- something that stops or delays progress
"The one blocker is the pending lender approval."
Deliverable -- a concrete item to be produced and handed over
"Each deliverable has an owner and a deadline."
Timeline -- the sequence of dates and deadlines
"The timeline shows closing by next Friday."
Highlight -- the single most important point to flag
"The highlight: we have raised thirty million so far."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Status
Update
Progress
Pending
Blocker
Deliverable
Timeline
Highlight

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda writes the weekly update for the stakeholders. She leads with the headline status and one highlight. To report progress, she uses the present perfect, because the result matters now: "We have completed due diligence", "the client has approved the timeline", "we have already booked the closing call", and "we have raised thirty million so far". For open items she uses "not... yet": "we have not closed the financing yet". She names the one blocker (the pending lender approval) and gives each deliverable an owner and a timeline.

1. Why does Eduarda write "We have completed due diligence" rather than "We completed it on Monday"?
A Because the present perfect is more formal.
B Because she reports a result that matters now, without an exact time.
C Because the task is not finished.
2. How does she report an open item?
A "We have not closed the financing yet."
B "We not closed the financing."
C "We closed the financing yet."
3. What does she give each deliverable?
A A salutation.
B An owner and a timeline.
C A quorum.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Use the present perfect (have/has + past participle) to report a result that matters now, without saying exactly when. Add already (done sooner than expected), yet (not done, in negatives), and so far / to date (up to now). Use o present perfect para um RESULTADO com relevancia agora, sem dizer quando: "We have completed it". Para um momento exato, use o past simple: "We completed it on Monday".

UseFormExample
Completed resulthave/has + p.p."We have completed due diligence."
Not finishedhave not + p.p. + yet"We have not closed it yet."
Up to nowhave + p.p. + so far"We have raised $30M so far."

Common Mistake:

"We have completed due diligence yesterday."

"We have completed due diligence." (no exact time)

Present perfect = resultado AGORA, sem tempo exato. Com um tempo exato (yesterday, on Monday), use o past simple.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"We have due diligence."
"The client approved the timeline."
"We have not closed the financing ."
"We have raised thirty million ."
"The one is the pending lender approval."
"Each deliverable has an owner and a ."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the steps of writing the status report in the correct order. Listen first.

? "She flagged the one blocker that was still pending."
? "She opened the report with the headline status."
? "She highlighted the key point for the partners and sent the draft."
? "She named a deliverable with an owner and a timeline."
? "She listed what the team had completed using the present perfect."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

We have completed due diligence.
We have not closed the financing yet.
The highlight: we are ahead of the timeline.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Due diligence is finished and it matters now. How do you report it?
A "We have completed due diligence on Monday."
B "We have completed due diligence."
C "We completing due diligence."
2. The financing is still open. What do you write?
A "We have not closed the financing yet."
B "We have closed the financing yet."
C "We not close the financing."
3. You open a status report. What comes first?
A The headline status and the highlight.
B The list of blockers, with no context.
C The sign-off.

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Think of a real project you are working on. In 3-4 sentences, dictate a status update: give the headline status, report two results in the present perfect ("We have..."), and flag one blocker or pending item. Use at least two key terms.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1 Headline status: the deal is on track. Status principal: o negócio está dentro do previsto.
2 We have completed due diligence. Concluímos a due diligence.
3 We have raised thirty million so far. Captamos trinta milhões até agora.
4 We have not closed the financing yet. Ainda não fechamos o financiamento.
5 The highlight: we are ahead of the timeline. O destaque: estamos adiantados em relação ao cronograma.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

The Writing Portfolio -- Review & Choosing the Right Format

Format-choice vocabulary + question word + to (decide whether to send an email; which format to use): match format, register, and tone to the audience
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key format-choice terms for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Audience -- the people who will read your writing
"Always start from your audience: who will read this?"
Purpose -- the reason you are writing
"What is the purpose: to ask, inform, or record?"
Format -- the type of document (email, memo, minutes...)
"Choose the format before you write a word."
Register -- the level of formality in the language
"A client needs a formal register; the team, a neutral one."
Tone -- the attitude or feeling of the message
"Match the tone to the reader: warm, not blunt."
Tailor -- to adjust the writing to a specific reader
"Tailor the length and tone to your audience."
Proofread -- to check carefully for errors before sending
"Proofread the text before you send it."
Revise -- to improve or rewrite after checking
"Revise it once before you circulate it."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with its definition.

Audience
Purpose
Format
Register
Tone
Tailor
Proofread
Revise

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Eduarda has built a writing portfolio: emails, memos, minutes, and status reports. The real skill is choosing the right format. She always starts from two questions: who is the audience, and what is the purpose? Then she decides whether to send an email or a memo, and she knows which format to use for a meeting record or a stakeholder update. The format sets the register: formal for a client, neutral for the team. She tailors the tone to each reader, and she knows when to write at all. Finally, she always proofreads the text and revises it once before sending.

1. After "decide", how does Eduarda talk about a yes/no choice of format?
A "decide whether she should send an email"
B "decide whether to send an email"
C "decide whether to sending an email"
2. What two questions does she ask first?
A Who is the audience, and what is the purpose.
B How long, and what color.
C When to send, and where to save.
3. What are her last two steps before sending?
A Print it and file it.
B Proofread it, then revise it once.
C Forward it and forget it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

After verbs like decide, know, ask, be sure, use a question word + to + verb to talk about a decision: whether to (a yes/no choice), which (+ noun) to (a choice of option), when / how to. No subject, no "should". Depois de decide/know/ask, use uma question word (whether/which/when/how) + TO + verbo base: "decide whether to send an email", "which format to use".

DecisionStructureExample
Yes / nowhether to + verb"Decide whether to send an email."
Choice of optionwhich (+ noun) to + verb"which format to use"
Time / waywhen / how to + verb"when to write a report"

Common Mistake:

"I must decide whether I should send an email or which format I should use."

"I must decide whether to send an email and which format to use."

Depois da question word, use so "to + verbo" — sem sujeito, sem "should".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"Decide to send an email or a memo."
"I am not sure which format to ."
"Always start from your : who will read this?"
"A client needs a formal ; the team, a neutral one."
"I the tone to each reader."
"I the text before I send it."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the steps of choosing a format in the correct order. Listen first.

? "She chose the right register and tone for the reader."
? "She first identified the audience and the purpose."
? "She revised it once and then sent it."
? "She tailored the length and proofread the text."
? "She decided which format to use."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Decide whether to send an email or a memo.
I know which format to use for each purpose.
I proofread it, then revise it once before sending.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You record what a board meeting decided. Which format?
A A status report.
B The minutes.
C A client email.
2. Which sentence is correct?
A "I do not know which format I should to use."
B "I do not know which format to use."
C "I do not know which format using."
3. Before writing anything, what should you decide first?
A The audience and the purpose.
B The font and the color.
C The sign-off.

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Think of something you need to write this week. In 3-4 sentences, dictate your decision: name the audience and purpose, say "I will decide whether to..." and "which format to use", and how you will tailor the tone. Use at least two key terms.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1 Who is the audience, and what is the purpose? Quem é o público, e qual é o objetivo?
2 Decide whether to send an email or a memo. Decida se deve enviar um e-mail ou um memorando.
3 I know which format to use for each purpose. Eu sei qual formato usar para cada objetivo.
4 I tailor the register and tone to the reader. Ajusto o registro e o tom ao leitor.
5 I proofread it, then revise it once before sending. Reviso os erros e depois reescrevo uma vez antes de enviar.

01
Who Is Eduarda Gabriel?

02

Joining the Conversation -- 27 slides
03
Numbers That Tell Stories

First Client Call
Review & Simulation -- All structures integrated -- 27 slides
06
Ice-Breaking in International Settings
Social English & question forms -- 27 slides
07
Turn-Taking: Interrupting & Yielding Politely
Discourse markers & interruption phrases -- 27 slides
08
Defending a Position: Arguing with Data
First conditional & opinion frames -- 27 slides
09
Summarizing & Clarifying in Real Time

10
Review & Simulation: Strategy Meeting

11
Chapter 11 -- Legal Framework Vocabulary

12

Modal verbs (could/would/might) -- 27 slides
13
Closing the Deal -- Signing & Contract Terms
First conditional (if / unless + will) -- 27 slides
14
Post-Closing -- Monitoring & Compliance
