15
Restructuring Outcomes -- Lessons Learned
Third conditional (if + past perfect, would have) -- 27 slides
16
Professional Emails -- Structure & Tone
Email formulas + formal register -- 27 slides
17
Deal Memos -- Concise Written Communication
Memo vocabulary + participle phrases for concision -- 27 slides
18
Meeting Minutes -- Recording Decisions & Actions
Minutes vocabulary + reported speech backshift -- 27 slides
19
Status Reports -- Updating Stakeholders in Writing
Status vocabulary + present perfect for results -- 27 slides
20
The Writing Portfolio -- Review & Choosing the Right Format
Format-choice vocabulary + whether to / which to -- 27 slides

Materiais Complementares -- Aula 1

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Lesson 1 — Who Is Eduarda Gabriel?

Série
Billions -- Season 1, Episode 1 (Showtime)

Watch the first episode and pay attention to how characters discuss deals and negotiate. Notice phrases like "walk me through", "the deal is...", and how they introduce themselves.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
Planet Money -- "The Economy Explained" (NPR)

Listen to one episode about business or finance. Focus on how the host introduces topics and experts introduce themselves.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x de velocidade primeiro.

Listen on Spotify ↗
TED Talk
"How to Speak so That People Want to Listen" -- Julian Treasure

Pay attention to Julian's speaking pace and clarity. Notice how he structures his points.

Dica: assista uma vez com legenda, uma vez sem.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 2 — Holding the Floor

Série
Succession -- Season 1, Episode 3 "Lifeboats" (HBO)

Notice how characters interrupt each other, take turns, and fight for the floor. Pay attention to phrases like "If I may..." and "Going back to...".

Dica: foque nas cenas de reunião do conselho.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Acquired -- "The Berkshire Hathaway Episode"

Listen to how the hosts transition between topics, ask clarifying questions, and build on each other's points.

Dica: anote 3 frases que eles usam para trocar de turno.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
"How to Interrupt Politely in English" -- English with Lucy

Practical guide to turn-taking in English meetings. Learn 5 essential phrases for joining conversations.

Dica: pratique as 5 frases do vídeo em voz alta.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Numbers That Tell Stories

Série
Billions -- Season 1, Episode 3 "YumTime" (Showtime)

Watch how Axe Capital analysts present numbers and discuss financial data. Focus on how they say large numbers and percentages.

Dica: pause e repita as frases com números.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
Planet Money -- "The Indicator" (NPR)

Short episodes (10 min) about financial data. Focus on how they describe economic trends and percentages.

Dica: anote 5 números que você ouvir e como foram ditos.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
"How to Present Data in English" -- Business English Pod

Professional data presentation phrases and structures. Learn how to describe charts and trends.

Dica: pratique as frases-modelo do vídeo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — The Deal Room

Série
Succession -- Season 2, Episode 1 "The Summer Palace" (HBO)

Watch how characters discuss deals, negotiations, and use business collocations. Focus on phrases like "close the deal", "reach an agreement".

Dica: anote 5 collocations que você ouvir.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Acquired -- "The Disney-Fox Deal" episode

Listen to how the hosts describe the M&A process -- due diligence, negotiations, closing. Note how they use present perfect.

Dica: liste os passos do deal na ordem em que são mencionados.

Listen on Spotify ↗
TED Talk
"What I Learned from 100 Days of Rejection" -- Jia Jiang

Not about finance, but about negotiation confidence. Watch how he handles rejection -- useful mindset for deal discussions.

Dica: anote 3 lições de negociação do vídeo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — First Client Call

Série
Billions -- Season 1, Episode 5 "The Good Life" (Showtime)

Full episode watching challenge. Focus on ALL the business vocabulary — deals, negotiations, numbers, meetings. Write down every phrase you recognize from our 4 lessons.

Dica: assista o episódio inteiro — este é o seu teste.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
How I Built This -- "Canva: Melanie Perkins" (NPR)

Listen to how Melanie describes pitching investors, raising capital, and negotiating deals. Note how she uses present perfect to describe her journey.

Dica: escreva uma linha do tempo do que ela já fez (has done).

Listen on Spotify ↗
Documentário
"Inside Job" (2010) -- primeiros 30 minutos

Watch the first 30 minutes and focus on financial vocabulary: leverage, margin, revenue, restructuring. This documentary uses real banker interviews.

Dica: liste 10 termos financeiros que você ouvir.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Lesson 6 — Ice-Breaking in International Settings

Série
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot" (USA Network)

Assista à cena de abertura no hotel: repare em como Harvey e Mike puxam conversa com estranhos com confiança. Note as perguntas sociais ("So, what do you do?") e como eles encontram common ground rapidamente, exatamente como nesta aula.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote cada pergunta social que ouvir (Do you...? Have you...? What...?).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
All Ears English -- "Business Small Talk & Socializing" series

Ouça um episódio da seção de small talk e networking. Lindsay e Michelle mostram como quebrar o gelo, manter a conversa e fazer perguntas naturais em inglês americano -- o foco exato da Aula 6.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada pergunta social que aparecer.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy -- "100 Small Talk Questions and Answers"

Vídeo com 100 perguntas e respostas de small talk para praticar. Repare na estrutura das perguntas (auxiliar antes do sujeito) e escolha 5 para usar no seu próximo evento. Conecta direto com a gramática de question forms da aula.

Dica: pause após cada pergunta, repita em voz alta e tente responder antes de ouvir a resposta.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Turn-Taking: Interrupting & Yielding Politely

Série
Succession -- Season 1, Episode 6: "Which Side Are You On?" (HBO)

Assista à cena da reunião do conselho (board): repare em como as pessoas se interrompem, seguram e cedem o turno sob pressão. Note quem domina e quem usa marcadores para entrar ("Sorry to jump in...", "If I could just..."). Contraste com a versão EDUCADA que você treinou nesta aula.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote cada vez que alguém interrompe. A frase foi educada ou agressiva?

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English -- Meetings & turn-taking series

Ouça um episódio sobre reuniões em inglês. Lindsay e Aubrey mostram frases naturais para interromper, segurar o turno e ceder a palavra em calls de trabalho -- exatamente o foco da Aula 7 (jump in, hold the floor, go ahead).

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada frase de turn-taking que aparecer.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Business English Pod -- "How to Interrupt in Business English Politely"

Vídeo com frases prontas para interromper e ceder a palavra em reuniões ("Sorry to jump in...", "Could I add something?", "Please, go ahead"). Conecta direto com os discourse markers e as frases de turn-taking da aula. Escolha 5 para usar na sua próxima call.

Dica: pause após cada frase, repita em voz alta e classifique a função (entrar / segurar / ceder / retomar).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Defending a Position: Arguing with Data

Filme
Margin Call (2011) -- a cena da reunião de emergência

Assista à reunião noturna dos executivos: cada um defende uma posição com DADOS e argumenta consequências ("If we sell first, we will survive"). Repare nos opinion frames, no upside/downside e nos condicionais — exatamente o foco da aula. John Tuld pede que tudo seja explicado de forma simples (back it up).

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada frase do tipo "If... , we will..." e cada vez que alguém pede para embasar um número (back up).

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English -- Opinions & disagreeing series

Ouça um episódio sobre dar opiniões e discordar em reuniões. Mostram opinion frames naturais ("In my view...", "The data suggests...") e como fazer push back com educação — o foco exato da Aula 8.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada opinion frame e tente montar um first conditional com ele.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
Business English Pod -- "BEP 327: Expressing Opinions in English"

Lição em vídeo com frases prontas para dar e defender opiniões em inglês ("In my view...", "I would argue that...", "The way I see it..."). Conecta direto com os opinion frames da aula. Escolha 5 para usar na sua próxima reunião.

Dica: pause após cada frase, repita em voz alta e combine com um first conditional ("In my view, if we wait, we will lose the deal").

Watch on Business English Pod ↗

Lesson 9 — Summarizing & Clarifying in Real Time

Filme
Apollo 13 (1995) -- as cenas do controle da missão

Assista às cenas do mission control: sob pressão, eles RESUMEM a situação em tempo real, reportam o que cada equipe disse ("They say...") e confirmam que todos entenderam ("Let me make sure I understand..."). É o foco exato da aula: summarizing & clarifying.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada vez que alguém resume ("So, to sum up...") ou reporta o que outro disse (reporting verbs).

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English -- Meetings & clarifying series

Ouça um episódio sobre fechar reuniões e checar entendimento. Mostram como resumir, parafrasear ("in other words...") e confirmar que todos estão "on the same page" -- o foco exato da Aula 9.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada frase de resumo e tente reportar o que ouviu com "She said that...".

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
Business English Pod -- "BEP 81c: Action Points and Wrapping Up"

Lição em vídeo sobre como fechar uma reunião: resumir os action items, atribuir tarefas e confirmar entendimento. Conecta direto com sum up, key takeaway, action item e "on the same page" da aula. Escolha 5 frases para usar na sua próxima reunião.

Dica: pause após cada frase, repita em voz alta e classifique a função (resumir / reportar / parafrasear / confirmar).

Watch on Business English Pod ↗

Lesson 10 — Review & Simulation: Strategy Meeting

Filme
Moneyball (2011) -- as reuniões do front office

Assista às cenas de reunião dos scouts e do Billy Beane: ele DEFENDE uma posição com dados ("the data suggests..."), as pessoas discordam e fazem push back, e ele resume a decisão. É o arco completo da aula: abrir, defender com dados, gerenciar turnos e fechar.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada opinion frame, cada first conditional e como ele resume ("So, here's the deal...").

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English -- Running a meeting series

Ouça um episódio sobre conduzir uma reunião do começo ao fim. Revisa abrir, dar opinião, gerenciar a fala e fechar com action items -- exatamente os 4 skills integrados na Aula 10.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x. A cada frase, identifique qual aula (6, 7, 8 ou 9) ela revisa.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
Business English Pod -- "BEP 81: Ending a Meeting and Action Points"

Lição em vídeo sobre como conduzir e fechar uma reunião: sinalizar o fim, resumir, atribuir action items e confirmar entendimento. Junta o que você praticou nas aulas 7 e 9. Grave a si mesmo aplicando 5 frases.

Dica: depois de assistir, faça o seu próprio recap de uma reunião real usando as frases.

Watch on Business English Pod ↗

Lesson 11 — Chapter 11 -- Legal Framework Vocabulary

Série
Suits -- episódios de reestruturação e litígio empresarial

Escolha um episódio em que a Pearson Hardman lida com uma empresa em dificuldade financeira. Observe como os advogados explicam processos legais na voz passiva ("the petition was filed", "the deal must be approved") e usam vocabulário como insolvent, creditors e bankruptcy.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada frase na passiva e tente reescrevê-la na ativa para sentir a diferença.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Bloomberg -- "The Pay Check" / restructuring & bankruptcy episodes

Ouça um episódio sobre reestruturação corporativa e Chapter 11. Os analistas descrevem o processo passo a passo, muito na voz passiva, e usam exatamente o vocabulário da Aula 11: file for bankruptcy, automatic stay, reorganization plan, court approval.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada frase na passiva e identifique o "be + particípio".

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
Investopedia -- "Chapter 11 Bankruptcy Explained"

Vídeo curto que explica o processo de Chapter 11 do início ao fim: petição, automatic stay, debtor in possession, reorganization plan e court approval. Linguagem clara e formal, ideal para fixar o vocabulário e a voz passiva da aula.

Dica: pause após cada etapa, repita a frase na passiva e classifique o tempo verbal (present / past / modal).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Creditor Negotiations -- Language of Compromise

Série
Billions -- cenas de negociacao e fechamento de acordos

Escolha uma cena em que as partes negociam termos de um acordo. Observe como os personagens suavizam propostas e pedidos com could, would e might, e como buscam common ground em vez de impor exigencias. Anote vocabulario como counteroffer, concession e settlement.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada proposta tentativa ("We could...", "Would you...") e tente reescrevê-la de forma mais direta para sentir a diferença de tom.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
Negotiate Anything -- episodes on concessions & reaching agreement

Ouça um episódio sobre como fazer concessões e chegar a um acordo. Os convidados usam exatamente a linguagem tentativa da aula (could/would/might) e termos como counteroffer, meet halfway, common ground e settlement.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada proposta educada e identifique o modal (could, would ou might).

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
'What's your proposal?' – Language of negotiations part 1 | Business of English #12 | ABC Australia

Vídeo curto sobre como negociar de forma colaborativa: fazer propostas com could, pedidos com would e abrir possibilidades com might. Mostra como buscar common ground e fechar um settlement sem soar agressivo.

Dica: pause após cada proposta, repita a frase e classifique o modal (could / would / might) e a função (proposta, pedido ou possibilidade).

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Closing the Deal -- Signing & Contract Terms

Série
Suits -- cenas de assinatura e fechamento de contratos

Escolha uma cena em que os personagens fecham um acordo e assinam o contrato. Observe os termos legais (terms and conditions, binding, breach, enforceable) e como eles declaram condicoes com if e unless. Anote como uma consequencia segue uma condicao.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Sempre que ouvir uma frase com "if" ou "unless", identifique qual e a condicao e qual e o resultado (will).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Negotiate Anything -- episodes on closing & contract terms

Ouça um episódio sobre como fechar um acordo e o papel dos termos contratuais. Os convidados usam o primeiro condicional o tempo todo (if/unless + will) e termos como binding, default e enforceable.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada frase condicional e confirme: presente depois de if/unless, will no resultado.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
BBC Learning English -- "First Conditional" (if / unless + will)

Vídeo curto que explica o primeiro condicional: como ligar uma condicao real (if/unless + presente) ao seu resultado (will + verbo base). Ideal para fixar a estrutura usada nesta aula de fechamento de contratos.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo, repita a frase e crie a sua propria versao sobre um contrato (ex: "If they sign, the deal will close").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Post-Closing -- Monitoring & Compliance

Série
Industry (HBO) -- cenas de monitoramento de risco e compliance

Escolha uma cena em que os analistas acompanham posicoes e reportam status ao chefe. Observe os termos (compliance, flag, escalate, track record) e como descrevem o que vem acontecendo desde uma data ("we have been watching...").

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Sempre que ouvir "have/has been + -ing", identifique desde quando a acao acontece (since/for).

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Goldman Sachs Exchanges -- episodes on credit & monitoring

Ouça um episódio sobre acompanhamento de credito e risco de portfolio. Os convidados frequentemente reportam tendencias em andamento ("we have been seeing...", "credit has been holding up since...") — exatamente o present perfect continuous desta aula.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Repita em voz alta cada frase com have/has been + -ing e diga desde quando (since/for).

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
BBC Learning English -- "Present Perfect Continuous" (have/has been + -ing)

Vídeo curto que explica o present perfect continuous: como relatar uma acao que comecou no passado e continua agora, usando since e for. Ideal para fixar a estrutura usada nesta aula de monitoramento e compliance.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo, repita a frase e crie a sua propria versao sobre um acordo (ex: "We have been monitoring the deal since March").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Restructuring Outcomes -- Lessons Learned

Série
Industry (HBO) -- cenas de revisao apos um negocio dar errado

Escolha uma cena em que a equipe revisa um deal que terminou e discute o que deu errado. Observe os termos (outcome, root cause, overlook) e como os personagens usam o terceiro condicional para falar do que teria sido diferente ("if we had pushed back, we would have...").

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Sempre que ouvir "if ... had ... would have ...", pare e repita a frase em voz alta.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Goldman Sachs Exchanges -- episodes on lessons from past deals

Ouça um episódio em que os convidados olham para tras e analisam negocios passados. Eles frequentemente usam hindsight e o terceiro condicional ("if rates had stayed low, the outcome would have been...") — exatamente a gramatica desta aula.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Sempre que ouvir uma frase com "would have", reconstrua a if-clause (if + had + participio).

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
How to use the third conditional - 6 Minute Grammar

Vídeo curto que explica o terceiro condicional: como imaginar um passado diferente que nao aconteceu, usando if + had + participio e would/could have + participio. Ideal para fixar a estrutura usada nesta aula de lessons learned.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo, repita a frase e crie a sua propria versao sobre um deal (ex: "If we had escalated sooner, we would have avoided the delay").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Professional Emails -- Structure & Tone

Série
Suits -- cenas de troca de emails e cartas formais entre escritorios

Escolha uma cena em que os advogados escrevem ou leem um email/carta formal a um cliente ou a parte contraria. Observe os termos (salutation, recipient, sign-off) e como eles suavizam pedidos ("I would appreciate...", "Could you please...") em vez de dar ordens.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Sempre que ouvir um pedido formal, pause e reescreva a versao "direta" para sentir a diferenca de tom.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- episodes on professional communication & email etiquette

Ouça um episódio sobre comunicacao profissional escrita. Os convidados explicam como manter um email conciso, com o tom certo, e como abrir e fechar uma mensagem — exatamente o foco desta aula.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Anote toda formula formal que ouvir ("I am writing to...", "Please find attached...") e use-a no seu proximo email real.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
English for Emails: Formal and informal language

Vídeo curto que mostra a estrutura de um email formal: salutation, purpose, pedido suavizado e sign-off. Ideal para fixar as formulas (could you please / I would appreciate / please find attached) usadas nesta aula.

Dica: pause após cada formula, repita em voz alta e reescreva um pedido seu para a forma formal (ex: "Send the deck" → "Please find attached the deck").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Deal Memos -- Concise Written Communication

Série
Industry -- cenas de bancos de investimento escrevendo memos e notas internas

Escolha uma cena em que os analistas escrevem ou discutem um memo, uma nota de deal ou um update interno. Observe como eles lideram com a recomendacao (headline) e cortam o que nao importa. Anote toda frase curta que poderia substituir uma longa.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Sempre que ouvir uma frase longa ("the deal that is closing..."), pause e reescreva a versao curta ("the deal closing...").

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- episodes on concise writing & communicating up to leadership

Ouça um episódio sobre escrever de forma concisa e comunicar uma recomendacao para a lideranca. Os convidados explicam como liderar com o ponto principal, cortar palavras e estruturar um documento curto — exatamente o foco desta aula.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Anote toda formula de abertura ("Bottom line...", "We recommend...") e use-a no seu proximo memo real.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
Get to the Point: Write Clearly, Concisely, and Professionally!

Vídeo curto sobre escrita concisa e como cortar palavras desnecessarias. Ideal para fixar as participle phrases (the deal closing / the terms agreed) e a estrutura de memo (headline, bullet points, rationale, scope) desta aula.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo, repita em voz alta e reescreva uma frase sua cortando "that is / that was" para a forma participial.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Meeting Minutes -- Recording Decisions & Actions

Série
Succession -- cenas de board meetings e decisoes corporativas

Escolha uma cena de reuniao de conselho. Observe quem fala, que decisoes sao tomadas, e imagine como voce escreveria isso nas minutes DEPOIS — reportando tudo no passado. Anote toda fala viva e a versao reportada (he said... was / would).

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Quando alguem disser "we will...", pause e escreva a versao da minute: "he said they would...".

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- episodes on running effective meetings & reporting decisions

Ouça um episódio sobre conduzir reunioes e registrar decisoes. Os convidados falam de quorum, action owners e como documentar o que foi decidido — exatamente o foco desta aula.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Anote toda decisao mencionada e reescreva como uma resolution reportada no passado.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
BBC Learning English -- "Reported speech" (backshift: said... was / would / could)

Vídeo curto sobre reported speech e o backshift de tempos verbais. Ideal para fixar a regra (is→was, will→would, can→could) que voce usa para escrever minutes nesta aula.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo, repita em voz alta e reporte uma fala sua aplicando o backshift ("The deal is on track" → "He said the deal was on track").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Status Reports -- Updating Stakeholders in Writing

Série
Billions -- cenas de updates de deal e reports de progresso para o chefe

Escolha uma cena em que um analista atualiza o chefe sobre o andamento de um deal. Observe como reportam o que JA foi feito ("we have closed...", "we have raised...") e o que ainda esta pendente. Anote toda frase em present perfect.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Sempre que ouvir "we have + participio", pause e classifique: resultado feito, ou item ainda pendente (not yet).

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- episodes on status updates & communicating progress to stakeholders

Ouça um episódio sobre comunicar progresso e atualizar stakeholders. Os convidados falam de status, blockers e como reportar resultados de forma clara — exatamente o foco desta aula.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Anote toda frase de resultado e reescreva em present perfect ("We have completed...").

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
BBC Learning English -- "Present perfect for results" (already / yet / so far)

Vídeo curto sobre o present perfect para resultados e os marcadores already, yet e so far. Ideal para fixar a gramatica que voce usa para escrever status reports nesta aula.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo, repita em voz alta e reporte um resultado seu em present perfect ("I have finished...", "I have not started... yet").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — The Writing Portfolio -- Review & Choosing the Right Format

Série
Mad Men -- cenas de escrita persuasiva e escolha de formato/tom para cada cliente

Escolha uma cena em que os personagens decidem COMO comunicar uma ideia (carta, memo, pitch). Observe como adaptam o registro e o tom ao publico. Pergunte-se: por que esse formato, e nao outro?

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Para cada mensagem, identifique o audience e o purpose, e diga qual formato voce usaria.

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- episodes on business writing, audience & choosing the right channel

Ouça um episódio sobre escrita profissional, publico e escolha do canal/formato certo. Os convidados explicam como decidir entre email, memo e report conforme o objetivo — o coracao desta aula de fechamento.

Dica: ouça a 0.75x primeiro. Anote toda frase de decisao ("whether to...", "which... to...") e use-a na sua proxima escolha de formato.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Vídeo
Writing Letters: formal & informal English

Vídeo curto sobre escolher o formato certo e escrever para o publico. Ideal para consolidar todo o bloco de Writing: email, memo, minutes, status report, e a decisao (whether to / which to) que une os quatro.

Dica: pause após cada dica, repita em voz alta e aplique a uma mensagem real sua: audience, purpose, formato, register, proofread, revise.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

The Dream Words The Code Context Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Chapter 1 — The Dream

Eduarda's Voice at the Table

From understanding every word to leading the conversation

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Let's Talk About You

"Hi Eduarda! How was your week at Houlihan Lokey?"
"Tell me about a deal you are currently working on."
"When was the last time you spoke English in a meeting?"
Your Promise

After this lesson, you will be able to...

Introduce yourself confidently on a restructuring call
Describe your role and responsibilities at Houlihan Lokey
Use present simple and present continuous accurately
Chapter 2 — Packing Words

Your Professional
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for investment banking self-introduction

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

The process of reorganizing a company's financial structure
Restructuring
The process of reorganizing a company's financial structure
"Houlihan Lokey is a leader in restructuring."
A business agreement or transaction between parties
Deal
A business agreement or transaction between parties
"Eduarda is working on a new deal."
A person in a similar position at another company
Counterpart
A person in a similar position at another company
"My counterpart in New York coordinates meetings."
To give professional guidance or recommendations
Advise
To give professional guidance or recommendations
"We advise clients on Chapter 11."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

At the present time; right now
Currently
At the present time; right now
"I am currently focused on a major case."
To actively participate or add value to a discussion
Contribute
To actively participate or add value to a discussion
"I want to contribute more in English calls."
A person or group with an interest in a company's decisions
Stakeholder
A person or group with an interest in a company's decisions
"The stakeholders approved the plan."
To explain something step by step
Walk Through
To explain something step by step
"Let me walk you through the deal structure."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Restructuring"Houlihan Lokey is a leader in restructuring."
Deal"Eduarda is working on a new deal."
Counterpart"My counterpart coordinates the meetings."
Advise"We advise clients on Chapter 11."
Currently"I am currently focused on a case."
Contribute"I want to contribute to the discussion."
Stakeholder"The stakeholders approved the plan."
Walk Through"Let me walk you through the structure."
Chapter 3 — The Code

Grammar Discovery

Look at the purple words. What do they have in common?

Eduarda works at Houlihan Lokey.
She advises clients on restructuring.
She is working on a new deal this month.
The team is preparing the creditor presentation.
TenseUseExample
Present SimpleFacts / RoutinesShe works at Houlihan Lokey.
Present ContinuousNow / TemporaryShe is working on a deal.
Grammar Rule

Present Simple vs Present Continuous

TenseStructureExample
Present SimpleSubject + verb(s)"She works at Houlihan Lokey."
Present ContinuousSubject + am/is/are + verb-ing"She is working on a deal."
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"She is working at Houlihan Lokey." (for permanent job)
"She works at Houlihan Lokey." (present simple for permanent facts)

Use Present Simple for permanent facts. Use Present Continuous for temporary or current actions.

Practice

Fill in: Simple or Continuous?

"Eduarda ___works at Houlihan Lokey."
"She ___advises clients on restructuring."
"Right now, the team ___is preparing a presentation."
"She ___coordinates with counterparts every week."
"This month, Eduarda ___is focusing on a Chapter 11 case."
"Stakeholders ___are reviewing the deal terms today."
Chapter 4 — Getting There

Eduarda on the
Restructuring Call

Time to use your vocabulary in real professional situations.

Dialogue

Meeting James

J
Good morning. I am James from the New York office. Are you joining the restructuring call today?
E
Yes, hi James. I am Eduarda from the São Paulo team.
J
Great to have you. What is your role on this deal?
E
I am an associate. I am currently responsible for the financial analysis.
J
Excellent. Could you walk us through the latest numbers?
E
Of course. Let me share my screen. The restructuring plan shows three main scenarios.
J
This is very helpful. Who are the main stakeholders on the Brazilian side?
E
The main stakeholders are the creditors and the company board. I advise them on the deal structure.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What is Eduarda currently responsible for?
The financial analysis.
2. Where is James from?
The New York office.
3. Who are the main stakeholders on the Brazilian side?
The creditors and the company board.
Artifact

Professional Email

From: eduarda.gabriel@hl.com
To: james.morrison@hl.com
Subject: Introduction — São Paulo Team

Dear James,

I am Eduarda Gabriel, an investment banking associate on the restructuring team in São Paulo. I am currently working on the Brazilian side of the cross-border deal.

I look forward to coordinating with your team on this project. Could you walk me through the timeline?

Best regards,
Eduarda Gabriel
Houlihan Lokey — São Paulo

1. What tense does Eduarda use to describe her job title?
Present Simple: "I am Eduarda Gabriel, an investment banking associate."
2. What tense does she use for the temporary project?
Present Continuous: "I am currently working on the Brazilian side."
3. What phrasal verb does she use to ask for an explanation?
"Walk through" — "Could you walk me through the timeline?"
Chapter 5 — Practice

Time to
Practice

Listening

Restructuring Update Call

Listen to a team member giving an update on a restructuring call.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What is the speaker's role?
An associate on the restructuring team
2. What is the team currently working on?
A cross-border restructuring deal
3. Who are the main stakeholders mentioned?
The creditors and the company board
Listening

Meeting Introduction

Listen to someone introducing themselves at a meeting.

0:000:00
Speed
1. How does the speaker introduce herself?
With name, role, and current project
2. What tense does the speaker use for their daily routine?
Present simple
Quick Fire

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A colleague asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"I am an investment banking associate at Houlihan Lokey. I advise clients on restructuring."
Detective

Spot the Error

"I am work at Houlihan Lokey."
"I work at Houlihan Lokey."
"She advise clients on restructuring."
"She advises clients on restructuring."
"I am currently work on a deal."
"I am currently working on a deal."
"The stakeholders is reviewing the plan."
"The stakeholders are reviewing the plan."
"My counterpart walk me through the numbers."
"My counterpart walks me through the numbers."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. You are on a video call. Introduce yourself to the team.
"Good morning. I am Eduarda Gabriel, an associate at Houlihan Lokey in São Paulo."
2. Describe what you do every day.
"I advise clients on restructuring and coordinate with our New York counterparts."
3. Talk about your current project.
"I am currently working on a Chapter 11 restructuring case."
4. Explain who the stakeholders are.
"The main stakeholders are the creditors, the board, and the advisory team."
5. Ask someone to explain the deal.
"Could you walk me through the deal structure, please?"
6. Offer to contribute to the discussion.
"I would like to contribute to this discussion. May I add something?"
Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Your Turn

Role-Play

Role-Play: Restructuring Call

You are joining a restructuring call for the first time. Introduce yourself to the US team. Use these keywords:
introduceassociateHoulihan LokeycurrentlyrestructuringSão Paulo
Role-Play

Role-Play: New Analyst

A new analyst joins your team. Explain your role and what the team does. Some keywords to guide you:
advisedealstakeholderscoordinate
Free Practice

Role-Play: Update Call

You are on a restructuring call. The lead asks for an update on the Brazilian side. Speak for 60 seconds — no script, no keywords.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7 — Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"I am Eduarda Gabriel. I work at Houlihan Lokey."
2
"I am currently working on a restructuring deal."
3
"Could you walk me through the deal structure?"
4
"I would like to contribute to this discussion."
5
"The main stakeholders have approved the plan."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself professionally on a call.
I can describe my role at Houlihan Lokey.
I can use present simple for facts and routines.
I can use present continuous for current projects.
I can use key vocabulary: restructuring, deal, counterpart.
I can ask someone to walk me through a topic.
I can offer to contribute to a discussion.
I feel more confident about speaking English at work.

Day 1 — Complete.

Next lesson: Holding the Floor — Joining the Conversation

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Holding the Floor

From listening silently to leading the conversation

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Warm-Up: Callback

"Last class, you introduced yourself. Can you do it again without reading?"
"What is one deal you are currently working on at Houlihan Lokey?"
"When someone is speaking in a call, how do you usually join the conversation?"
Your Promise

After this lesson, you will be able to...

Politely interrupt and take turns in a meeting
Ask clear questions using present simple
Share your perspective with confidence
Chapter 2 — Packing Words

Words 1-4

To politely break into a conversation to add your point
INTERRUPT
To politely break into a conversation to add your point
"Sorry to interrupt, but I have an update on the deal."
To make something clearer or easier to understand
CLARIFY
To make something clearer or easier to understand
"Could you clarify the recovery timeline?"
To explain something in more detail
ELABORATE
To explain something in more detail
"Could you elaborate on the restructuring proposal?"
A list of topics to be discussed in a meeting
AGENDA
A list of topics to be discussed in a meeting
"The first item on the agenda is the creditor proposal."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To continue or check on something discussed earlier
FOLLOW UP
To continue or check on something discussed earlier
"I will follow up with the legal team on Friday."
A particular way of thinking about something
PERSPECTIVE
A particular way of thinking about something
"From my perspective, the deal needs more time."
A formal suggestion or plan presented for consideration
PROPOSAL
A formal suggestion or plan presented for consideration
"The creditors submitted a new proposal yesterday."
General agreement among a group of people
CONSENSUS
General agreement among a group of people
"Do we have a consensus on the next steps?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Interrupt"Sorry to interrupt, but I have an update."
Clarify"Could you clarify the recovery timeline?"
Elaborate"Could you elaborate on the proposal?"
Agenda"The first item on the agenda is the proposal."
Follow Up"I will follow up with the legal team."
Perspective"From my perspective, the deal needs time."
Proposal"The creditors submitted a new proposal."
Consensus"Do we have a consensus on the next steps?"
Chapter 3 — The Code

Grammar Discovery

Look at the purple words. What patterns do you see?

Do you agree with the restructuring proposal?
Does the team have a consensus on the timeline?
Could I add something to the agenda?
If I may, I would like to elaborate on that.
StructureFormulaExample
Present Simple Q (do)Do + subj + base verbDo you agree?
Present Simple Q (does)Does + subj + base verbDoes the team have...?
Turn-takingCould I / If I may / Sorry to interruptCould I add something?
Grammar Rule

Present Simple Questions & Turn-Taking

StructureFormulaExample
Do + I/you/we/theyDo + subject + base verb?"Do you agree with the proposal?"
Does + he/she/itDoes + subject + base verb?"Does the client need more time?"
Could I...?Polite request for the floor"Could I add something?"
If I may...Formal introduction of opinion"If I may, I would like to elaborate."
Sorry to interrupt...Polite interruption"Sorry to interrupt, but..."
Going back to...Return to previous topic"Going back to what you said..."
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"Do she agree with the proposal?"
"Does she agree with the proposal?"
"Could I to add something?"
"Could I add something?"
Practice

Fill in: Questions & Turn-Taking

"___Do you agree with the restructuring proposal?"
"___Does the team have a consensus on the timeline?"
"___Could I add something to the agenda?"
"___If I may, I would like to elaborate on that."
"I will ___follow up with the legal team before the next call."
"From my ___perspective, the proposal needs more time."
Chapter 4 — Getting There

Joining the
Meeting

Time to use your turn-taking phrases in real professional situations.

Dialogue

The Restructuring Call

J
Alright, let us go through the agenda. First item — the creditor proposal.
E
If I may, I would like to share an update from the Brazilian side.
J
Please go ahead, Eduarda.
E
The creditors are asking us to elaborate on the recovery timeline.
J
I see. Do we have a consensus on the timeline internally?
E
Not yet. I think we need to clarify our position before the next call.
J
Good point. Could you follow up with the legal team on that?
E
Of course. I will follow up and share my perspective by Friday.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. How does Eduarda ask for the floor?
"If I may, I would like to share an update."
2. What does James ask about consensus?
"Do we have a consensus on the timeline internally?"
3. What will Eduarda follow up on?
She will follow up with the legal team and share her perspective by Friday.
Artifact

Follow-up Email

From: eduarda.gabriel@hl.com
To: james.morrison@hl.com
Subject: Follow-up — Creditor Timeline Discussion

Dear James,

Following up on today's call, I would like to elaborate on the creditor timeline. From my perspective, we need to clarify two key points before reaching consensus.

Could we add this to next week's agenda?

Best regards,
Eduarda Gabriel
Houlihan Lokey — São Paulo

1. What turn-taking phrase does Eduarda use to start the email?
"Following up" — a variation of "follow up"
2. What does Eduarda want to add to next week?
She wants to add the creditor timeline discussion to next week's agenda.
3. How many Lesson 2 vocabulary words can you find in this email?
Five: elaborate, perspective, clarify, consensus, agenda.
Chapter 5 — Practice

Meeting Update Call

Listen to a team member using turn-taking phrases in a meeting.

0:000:00
Speed
1. How does the speaker interrupt politely?
"Sorry to interrupt, but..." or "If I may..."
2. What does the speaker want to clarify?
The creditor timeline and next steps
3. Does the team reach consensus?
Not yet — they agree to follow up next week
Listening

Turn-Taking in Action

Listen to how people take turns in a professional meeting.

0:000:00
Speed
1. Which turn-taking phrases did you hear?
"Could I add something?", "Going back to what you said..."
2. How does the speaker share their perspective?
"From my perspective..." followed by their opinion
Quick Fire

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
The team is discussing something you disagree with. How do you politely interrupt?
"Sorry to interrupt, but I would like to share a different perspective."
Detective

Spot the Error

"Could I to add something?"
"Could I add something?"
"Do you agrees with the proposal?"
"Do you agree with the proposal?"
"I would like elaborate on this."
"I would like to elaborate on this."
"Let me to clarify what I mean."
"Let me clarify what I mean."
"We need follow up on the agenda."
"We need to follow up on the agenda."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. Someone is discussing a point you disagree with. Politely interrupt.
"Sorry to interrupt, but I would like to share a different perspective."
2. A colleague said something you did not understand. Ask for clarification.
"Could you clarify what you mean by that?"
3. You want to add your opinion to the discussion. Ask for the floor.
"If I may, I would like to add something to this point."
4. The meeting is ending without clear next steps. Suggest a follow-up.
"Before we end, could I suggest we follow up on the timeline next week?"
5. Someone asks if the team agrees. You agree. What do you say?
"Yes, from my perspective, we have reached a consensus on this."
6. You want to go back to a previous topic. What do you say?
"Going back to what you said about the proposal, I think we need to elaborate on the numbers."
Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Your Turn

Role-Play

Role-Play: Restructuring Call

You are in a weekly restructuring call. The lead is talking about the creditor proposal. You have new information. Politely interrupt and share your update.
interruptproposalclarifyperspectivefollow up
Role-Play

Role-Play: Debating Proposals

The team is debating two proposals for the restructuring timeline. Share your perspective and try to build consensus.
perspectiveconsensuselaborateagenda
Free Practice

Role-Play: Lead the Meeting

Full meeting simulation — lead 2 minutes of the agenda. No script.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7 — Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"Could I add something to this point?"
2
"If I may, I would like to share my perspective."
3
"Sorry to interrupt, but I think we need to clarify this."
4
"Going back to what you said about the deal..."
5
"Do we have a consensus on this proposal?"
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can politely interrupt in a meeting.
I can ask for the floor using turn-taking phrases.
I can ask clear questions using do/does.
I can use vocabulary: interrupt, clarify, elaborate, consensus.
I can suggest a follow-up before ending a discussion.
I can share my perspective on a deal.
I can reference a previous point in the conversation.
I feel more confident about joining conversations in English calls.

Floor Holder

You can now join any conversation with confidence.

Day 2 — Complete.

Next lesson: Numbers That Tell Stories — Financial Data in English

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Numbers That Tell Stories

From reading spreadsheets to presenting data with confidence

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Let's Warm Up

"Last class we practiced joining conversations. What phrases do you remember?"
"How often do you present financial data in English at work?"
"What numbers are important in your current deal at Houlihan Lokey?"
Today You Will...

Today's Promise

1
Say large numbers and percentages fluently
2
Present financial data clearly in a meeting
3
Compare results using professional language
Chapter 2 — Packing Words

Financial Vocabulary

the total income generated by a business
REVENUE
the total income generated by a business
"Houlihan Lokey's restructuring revenue grew by twelve percent this year."
to decrease in value or amount
DECLINE
to decrease in value or amount
"The company's revenue declined by eight percent in Q2."
an approximate calculation or judgment
ESTIMATE
an approximate calculation or judgment
"We estimate the deal value at approximately two hundred million dollars."
a prediction of future trends based on data
FORECAST
a prediction of future trends based on data
"The forecast shows a recovery in the second half of the year."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words

Financial Vocabulary

the use of borrowed money to increase potential returns
LEVERAGE
the use of borrowed money to increase potential returns
"The company's leverage ratio is higher than the industry benchmark."
the difference between costs and selling price; profit margin
MARGIN
the difference between costs and selling price; profit margin
"The operating margin improved from fifteen to twenty percent."
a three-month period in a financial year (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4)
QUARTER
a three-month period in a financial year
"Revenue in the third quarter exceeded our forecast."
a standard or reference point for comparison
BENCHMARK
a standard or reference point for comparison
"We use industry benchmarks to evaluate the deal."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Quick Review

REVENUE
DECLINE
ESTIMATE
FORECAST
LEVERAGE
MARGIN
QUARTER
BENCHMARK

Can you define each word and give an example?

Chapter 3 — The Code

Discover the Pattern

"Revenue declined by eight percent in Q2."
"We estimate the value at approximately two hundred million."
"The margin is higher than the industry benchmark."
"Compared to last quarter, leverage increased by three points."

How do we talk about changes in numbers?

The Rule

Numbers & Comparisons

PatternStructureExample
Increaseincreased / grew / rose BY X percent"Revenue grew by 12%"
Decreasedeclined / fell / dropped BY X percent"Costs fell by 8%"
Comparisonhigher / lower THAN, compared TO"Higher than the benchmark"
Approximationapproximately / about / roughly + number"Approximately $200M"
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"Revenue declined in fifteen percent."
"Revenue declined by fifteen percent."
"The margin is more higher than last year."
"The margin is higher than last year."
Practice

Fill in: Financial Data

"Revenue ___declined by eight percent in Q3."
"We ___estimate the deal value at approximately two hundred million."
"The margin is ___higher than the industry benchmark."
"___Compared to last quarter, leverage increased."
"The ___forecast shows revenue growing by five percent."
"The operating ___margin improved from fifteen to twenty percent."
Chapter 4 — Getting There

Presenting
the Numbers

Time to use your financial vocabulary in real professional situations.

Dialogue

The Q3 Data Call

J
Eduarda, could you walk us through the Q3 numbers?
E
Of course. Revenue in the third quarter was approximately one hundred and fifty million.
J
How does that compare to Q2?
E
Compared to Q2, revenue declined by eight percent. However, the operating margin improved.
J
What is the margin now?
E
The margin increased from fifteen to twenty percent. That is higher than the industry benchmark.
J
And the forecast for Q4?
E
We estimate a recovery. The forecast shows revenue growing by approximately five percent.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What was Q3 revenue?
Approximately one hundred and fifty million ($150M).
2. How does the margin compare to the industry benchmark?
The margin (20%) is higher than the industry benchmark.
3. What is the forecast for Q4?
Revenue is expected to grow by approximately five percent.
Artifact

Financial Summary

Quarterly Financial Summary
Company: [Confidential] | Period: Q3 2026
Prepared by: Eduarda Gabriel -- Houlihan Lokey
Revenue: $150M (↓ 8% vs Q2)
Operating Margin: 20% (↑ from 15%)
Leverage Ratio: 4.2x (above benchmark: 3.5x)
Deal Value Est.: ~$200M
Q4 Forecast: +5% revenue growth
STATUS: UNDER RESTRUCTURING
1. What happened to revenue compared to Q2?
Revenue declined by 8% compared to Q2.
2. Is the leverage ratio above or below the benchmark?
Above the benchmark (4.2x vs 3.5x).
3. How many Lesson 3 vocabulary words can you find?
Six: revenue, margin, leverage, benchmark, estimate, forecast.
Chapter 5 — Practice

Data Presentation

Listen to a financial data presentation in a meeting.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What numbers did you hear?
$150M revenue, 8% decline, 20% margin, 5% growth forecast
2. What comparison was made?
Margin compared to the industry benchmark
3. What is the forecast?
Revenue growing by approximately 5% in Q4
Listening

Financial Comparisons

Listen to how financial data is compared across periods.

0:000:00
Speed
1. Which comparison phrases did you hear?
"Compared to", "higher than", "while X increased, Y declined"
2. What was the key trend described?
Revenue declined but margin improved
Quick Fire

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Tell me: what is one hundred and fifty million in numbers?
"$150,000,000 or $150M"
Detective

Spot the Error

"Revenue declined in fifteen percent."
"Revenue declined by fifteen percent."
"The margin is more higher than Q2."
"The margin is higher than Q2."
"We estimate the deal in approximately 200 million."
"We estimate the deal at approximately 200 million."
"Compare to last quarter, results improved."
"Compared to last quarter, results improved."
"The forecast show a recovery in Q4."
"The forecast shows a recovery in Q4."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. Tell me: what is one hundred and fifty million in numbers?
"One hundred and fifty million dollars, or one-five-zero million."
2. Revenue fell from $200M to $180M. Describe the change.
"Revenue declined by ten percent, or twenty million dollars."
3. The margin went from 15% to 20%. How do you describe this?
"The margin increased by five percentage points, from fifteen to twenty percent."
4. Compare two quarters: Q2 revenue was $180M, Q3 was $150M.
"Compared to Q2, Q3 revenue was lower. It declined by approximately seventeen percent."
5. Present an estimate for a deal.
"We estimate the deal value at approximately two hundred million dollars."
6. Describe the forecast for next quarter.
"The forecast shows revenue growing by approximately five percent in Q4."
Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Your Turn

Role-Play

Role-Play: Present Q3 Numbers

Present the Q3 numbers to the team. Include revenue, the decline compared to Q2, the margin improvement, and the forecast for Q4.
revenuedeclinedpercentmarginbenchmarkestimate
Role-Play

Role-Play: Compare Quarters

Compare Q2 and Q3 results. Explain the revenue trend, margin changes, and what the forecast shows for Q4.
compared tohigher thanforecastquarter
Free Practice

Role-Play: Full Presentation

Full financial presentation -- 2 minutes, no script. Present quarterly results to the team.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7 — Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"Revenue increased by fifteen percent in Q3."
2
"Compared to last quarter, the margin is higher."
3
"We estimate the deal value at approximately two hundred million."
4
"The forecast shows a decline in the second half."
5
"Let me walk you through the numbers."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can say large numbers in English fluently.
I can describe percentages and changes.
I can compare financial data using "compared to" and "higher than".
I can present quarterly results clearly.
I can use vocabulary: revenue, margin, forecast, benchmark.
I can estimate values using "approximately".
I can describe trends (increase, decline, recovery).
I feel more confident presenting financial data in English.

Data Storyteller

You can now present financial data with confidence and clarity.

Day 3 — Complete.

Next lesson: The Deal Room — Restructuring Vocabulary Mastery

Chapter 1 — The Dream

The Deal Room

From knowing deal words to using them like a dealmaker

Chapter 1 — The Dream

Let's Warm Up

"Last class we talked about numbers. What was Houlihan Lokey's Q3 revenue?"
"Can you compare Q2 and Q3 results from memory?"
"Have you ever presented financial data in English at work?"
Today You Will...

Today's Promise

1
Use professional collocations naturally (close a deal, file for bankruptcy...)
2
Talk about completed actions using present perfect
3
Discuss a restructuring case with confidence
Chapter 2 — Packing Words

Deal Collocations

to finalize a business agreement
CLOSE A DEAL
to finalize a business agreement
"We closed the deal with the creditors last Friday."
to formally request legal protection from creditors
FILE FOR BANKRUPTCY
to formally request legal protection from creditors
"The company has filed for bankruptcy under Chapter 11."
to come to a mutual decision after negotiation
REACH AN AGREEMENT
to come to a mutual decision after negotiation
"The parties have reached an agreement on the payment terms."
to investigate a company before a transaction
CONDUCT DUE DILIGENCE
to investigate a company before a transaction
"We have conducted due diligence on the target company."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words

Deal Collocations

to formally present a plan for consideration
SUBMIT A PROPOSAL
to formally present a plan for consideration
"The advisory team has submitted a proposal to the board."
to discuss conditions until both sides agree
NEGOTIATE TERMS
to discuss conditions until both sides agree
"Eduarda has negotiated the terms with the US counterparts."
to obtain funding from investors or lenders
RAISE CAPITAL
to obtain funding from investors or lenders
"The company needs to raise capital to fund the restructuring."
to publish or release a formal document
ISSUE A REPORT
to publish or release a formal document
"We have issued the preliminary report to all stakeholders."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Quick Review

CLOSE A DEAL
FILE FOR BANKRUPTCY
REACH AN AGREEMENT
CONDUCT DUE DILIGENCE
SUBMIT A PROPOSAL
NEGOTIATE TERMS
RAISE CAPITAL
ISSUE A REPORT

Can you define each collocation and give an example?

Chapter 3 — The Code

Discover the Pattern

"We have closed similar deals before."
"The company has filed for bankruptcy."
"Have you conducted due diligence yet?"
"They have not reached an agreement."

What pattern do you see? When do we use this structure?

The Rule

Present Perfect

FormStructureExample
AffirmativeSubject + have/has + past participle"We have closed the deal."
NegativeSubject + have/has not + past participle"We have not reached an agreement."
QuestionHave/Has + subject + past participle?"Have you submitted the proposal?"
Time markers: already, yet, just, recently, since, for, ever, never
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"We have closed the deal last Friday."
"We closed the deal last Friday." (specific time = past simple)
"She has negotiate the terms."
"She has negotiated the terms."
Practice

Fill in: Present Perfect

"We ___have reached an agreement with the creditors."
"The company ___filed for bankruptcy last month." (past simple!)
"Eduarda ___has negotiated the terms for three weeks."
"Have you ___conducted due diligence yet?"
"The team has already ___submitted the proposal."
"They have not ___raised enough capital yet."
Chapter 4 — Getting There

The Deal
Discussion

Time to use your deal collocations in real professional situations.

Dialogue

Deal Status Call

J
Eduarda, what is the status of the Brazilian restructuring case?
E
We have made good progress. The company has filed for Chapter 11.
J
Have you conducted due diligence yet?
E
Yes, we have already conducted due diligence. The report is ready.
J
And the creditor negotiations?
E
We have negotiated the main terms, but we have not reached a final agreement yet.
J
When do you expect to close the deal?
E
We have submitted a proposal. If they accept, we could close the deal by the end of the quarter.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. Has the company filed for bankruptcy?
Yes, the company has filed for Chapter 11.
2. Have they reached a final agreement?
No, they have not reached a final agreement yet.
3. What has the team submitted?
They have submitted a proposal to the creditors.
Artifact

Deal Status Email

From: eduarda.gabriel@hl.com
To: restructuring-team@hl.com
Subject: Deal Status Update — Q3

Dear team,

I am writing to update you on the Brazilian restructuring case.

We have conducted due diligence and have issued the preliminary report. The company has filed for Chapter 11 protection.

We have negotiated the main terms with creditors, but have not reached a final agreement yet. We have submitted a revised proposal and expect a response by Friday.

Next steps: We need to raise additional capital before we can close the deal.

Best regards,
Eduarda Gabriel

1. How many present perfect sentences can you find?
Six: have conducted, have issued, has filed, have negotiated, have not reached, have submitted.
2. What has NOT been completed yet?
They have not reached a final agreement yet. They also need to raise capital and close the deal.
3. How many collocations from Lesson 4 can you find?
Seven: conducted due diligence, issued a report, filed for bankruptcy, negotiated terms, reached an agreement, submitted a proposal, close the deal, raise capital.
Chapter 5 — Practice

Deal Update

Listen to a deal status update in a team call.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What collocations did you hear?
Filed for bankruptcy, conducted due diligence, submitted a proposal, negotiated terms
2. What has been completed?
Due diligence has been conducted, a proposal has been submitted
3. What still needs to happen?
They need to reach an agreement and close the deal
Listening

Creditor Negotiation

Listen to a creditor negotiation update.

0:000:00
Speed
1. Which present perfect sentences did you hear?
"Have negotiated", "have not reached", "have submitted"
2. What is the current status?
Terms have been negotiated but no final agreement yet
Quick Fire

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Tell me about a deal your team has completed recently.
"We have closed a restructuring deal with Brazilian creditors."
Detective

Spot the Error

"We have closed the deal last Friday."
"We closed the deal last Friday." (specific time = past simple)
"She has negotiate the terms."
"She has negotiated the terms."
"Have you conduct due diligence yet?"
"Have you conducted due diligence yet?"
"They have already reach an agreement."
"They have already reached an agreement."
"The company has file for bankruptcy."
"The company has filed for bankruptcy."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. Tell me about a deal your team has completed recently.
"We have closed a restructuring deal with Brazilian creditors."
2. Has the company filed for bankruptcy?
"Yes, the company has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection."
3. What due diligence have you conducted?
"We have conducted financial and legal due diligence on the target company."
4. Have you reached an agreement with the creditors?
"Not yet. We have negotiated the main terms but have not reached a final agreement."
5. What has the team submitted to the board?
"We have submitted a revised proposal with three restructuring scenarios."
6. Describe the capital situation.
"The company needs to raise approximately fifty million dollars. We have already issued a report to potential investors."
Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Your Turn

Role-Play

Role-Play: Case Update

You are updating a US counterpart on a restructuring case. Walk them through what has been done and what still needs to happen.
conductedsubmittednegotiatedfiledreachedclosed
Role-Play

Role-Play: Creditor Meeting

A creditor asks about the deal status. Explain using present perfect for completed actions and present simple for next steps.
due diligenceproposalagreementcapital
Free Practice

Role-Play: Full Deal Discussion

Full deal discussion -- 2 minutes, no script. Present a complete case update to the team.

Chapter 7 — Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"We have closed the deal with the creditors."
2
"The company has filed for bankruptcy under Chapter 11."
3
"Have you conducted due diligence yet?"
4
"We have not reached an agreement yet."
5
"The team has already submitted the proposal."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can use professional collocations: close a deal, file for bankruptcy.
I can form present perfect sentences correctly.
I can talk about completed and incomplete actions.
I can use time markers: already, yet, just, since.
I can distinguish present perfect from past simple.
I can describe a deal status using restructuring vocabulary.
I can write a deal update email.
I feel confident discussing restructuring cases in English.

Deal Maker

You can now discuss restructuring deals with confidence and precision.

Day 4 — Complete.

Your First Client Call

Everything you have learned — in one conversation

Review Callback

"We have covered 4 lessons. What vocabulary do you remember?"
"Name three collocations from last class."
"What grammar structures have you practiced so far?"
Your Promise

After this lesson, you will be able to...

Use all vocabulary from Lessons 1-4 in a real context
Switch between grammar structures naturally
Complete a full 20-minute client call simulation
Chapter 2 — Packing Words

How Many Do You Remember?

Restructuring
Reorganizing a company's structure
Counterpart
Person in similar role at another org
Stakeholder
Person with interest in a business
Walk Through
To explain step by step
Interrupt
To politely break into a conversation
Consensus
General agreement among a group
Follow Up
To continue or check on something later
Perspective
A personal point of view
Revenue
Income from business activities
Forecast
Prediction about future performance
Leverage
Borrowed money used to invest
Benchmark
Standard for comparison
Close a Deal
To finalize a business agreement
Due Diligence
Investigation before a transaction
Negotiate Terms
To discuss conditions until agreed
Submit a Proposal
To formally present a plan

0 / 16 words revealed

Chapter 2 — Packing Words

Grammar Summary

Structure Example
Present Simple"She works at Houlihan Lokey."
Present Continuous"She is currently negotiating."
Turn-taking"If I may, I would like to..."
Present Perfect"We have conducted due diligence."

Which structure would you use when...

1. Describing your daily responsibilities?
Present Simple: "I advise clients on restructuring."
2. Talking about a project in progress?
Present Continuous: "I am currently working on a deal."
3. Joining a conversation politely?
Turn-taking: "If I may, I would like to add..."
4. Reporting what has been completed?
Present Perfect: "We have submitted the proposal."
Chapter 3 — The Code

Grammar Integration

Identifying structures in context

Chapter 3 — The Code

Identify the Structure

"She works at Houlihan Lokey."
Present Simple — facts and routines
"She is currently negotiating the terms."
Present Continuous — temporary action in progress
"Have you conducted due diligence yet?"
Present Perfect — completed action with current relevance
"Revenue declined by eight percent."
Past Simple + comparison preposition "by"
Chapter 3 — The Code

Mix & Match

"She ___ (work) at Houlihan Lokey." → works
"The team ___ currently ___ (negotiate) the terms." → is ... negotiating
"___ you ___ (conduct) due diligence yet?" → Have ... conducted
"Revenue ___ (decline) ___ eight percent." → declined ... by
"If I ___, I would like to elaborate." → may
"We have ___ (submit) the proposal." → submitted
Chapter 4 — Getting There

The Client Call

A full simulation with all structures integrated

Full Client Call Simulation

The Restructuring Call

J
James

"Good morning, everyone. Let us start with introductions. Eduarda, please go ahead."

E
Eduarda

"Good morning. I am Eduarda Gabriel, an associate on the restructuring team at Houlihan Lokey, based in São Paulo."

J
James

"Eduarda has been leading the financial analysis on this case. Eduarda, could you walk us through the latest numbers?"

E
Eduarda

"Of course. Revenue in Q3 declined by eight percent compared to Q2. However, the operating margin has improved from fifteen to twenty percent."

J
James

"What about the restructuring timeline?"

E
Eduarda

"We have conducted due diligence and have submitted a proposal to the creditors. We are currently negotiating the final terms."

J
James

"Have we reached a consensus internally?"

E
Eduarda

"Not yet. If I may, I would like to elaborate on two key points. First, the leverage ratio is still higher than the benchmark."

J
James

"Good point. What do you estimate for Q4?"

E
Eduarda

"The forecast shows revenue growing by approximately five percent. If the creditors accept our proposal, we could close the deal by the end of the quarter."

Dialogue Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

1. What happened to revenue in Q3?
Revenue declined by eight percent compared to Q2.
2. What has Eduarda's team already done?
They have conducted due diligence and submitted a proposal.
3. What does the forecast show for Q4?
Revenue growing by approximately five percent.
Client Briefing Email

Post-Call Summary

From: Eduarda Gabriel <e.gabriel@hl.com>
To: James Morrison <j.morrison@hl.com>
Subject: Client Call Summary — Restructuring Case Update

Dear James,

Thank you for the productive call this morning. Below is a summary of the key points discussed.

Current Status: Revenue in Q3 declined by eight percent compared to Q2. However, the operating margin has improved from fifteen to twenty percent, which is higher than the industry benchmark.

Completed Actions: We have conducted due diligence on the target company and have submitted a proposal to the creditors. We have negotiated the main terms, but we have not reached a final agreement yet.

Next Steps: We are currently negotiating the final terms. The forecast shows revenue growing by approximately five percent in Q4. I will follow up with the legal team and issue a report by Friday.

Please let me know if you need any additional information.

Best regards,
Eduarda Gabriel
Associate, Restructuring | Houlihan Lokey

Chapter 5 — Practice

Listening Challenge

0:00 / 0:00
1. What role does the speaker describe?
An associate on the restructuring team at Houlihan Lokey.
2. What has been submitted?
A proposal to the creditors.
Chapter 5 — Practice

Listening Two

0:00 / 0:00
1. By how much did revenue decline?
Eight percent compared to Q2.
2. What does the forecast show for Q4?
Revenue growing by approximately five percent.
Quick Fire

Integration Challenge

Question 1 of 6

Introduce yourself and describe your current project.

"Good morning. I am Eduarda Gabriel, an associate at Houlihan Lokey. I am currently working on a restructuring deal."

0 / 6

Spot the Error

Find the Mistakes

0 / 5 errors found

"She work at Houlihan Lokey."
"She works at Houlihan Lokey." (Present Simple: -s for he/she/it)
"She is work on a deal currently."
"She is currently working on a deal." (Present Continuous: be + verb-ing)
"We have conduct due diligence."
"We have conducted due diligence." (Present Perfect: have + past participle)
"Revenue declined in eight percent."
"Revenue declined by eight percent." (Use "by" with percentages)
"I want say something about the forecast."
"If I may, I would like to elaborate on the forecast." (Polite turn-taking)
Oral Drilling

Say It Right

1. Introduce yourself and state your role.

2. Present Q3 revenue data.

3. Describe what your team has done.

4. Politely interrupt with new forecast data.

5. Ask about consensus and follow-up.

6. Give a complete deal status update.

Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Your Turn

From guided practice to full simulation

Guided Practice

Role-Play: Client Introduction

Scenario:

"You are joining a client call for the first time. Introduce yourself, explain your role, and describe what your team is currently working on."

associate Houlihan Lokey advise restructuring currently deal
Semi-free Practice

Role-Play: Data Presentation

Scenario:

"Present Q3 financial data to the client. Then answer their questions about what has been done and what the next steps are."

revenue declined margin conducted submitted forecast
Free Practice

Full Simulation

FULL SIMULATION — Lead a 5-minute client call. Include: introduction, data presentation, deal status update, and next steps. No script, no keywords.

Chapter 7 — Wrap-Up

Master Card

1
"I am Eduarda Gabriel, an associate at Houlihan Lokey."
2
"I am currently working on a restructuring deal."
3
"Could you walk me through the deal structure?"
4
"If I may, I would like to share my perspective."
5
"Revenue declined by eight percent compared to Q2."
6
"We have conducted due diligence and submitted the proposal."
7
"Have we reached a consensus on the next steps?"
8
"The forecast shows revenue growing by approximately five percent."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself professionally on a client call.
I can describe my role using present simple.
I can talk about current projects using present continuous.
I can politely interrupt and take turns in meetings.
I can present financial data with numbers and percentages.
I can compare results using "compared to" and "higher than".
I can describe completed actions using present perfect.
I can use professional collocations: close a deal, conduct due diligence.
I can lead a complete client call in English.
I feel significantly more confident speaking English at work.

Client Call Champion

You can now lead a complete client call in English with confidence and precision.

COMPLETE

5 lessons. 32 words. 4 grammar structures. You are ready.
